Tafseer:
'Khalaqa minha zawjaha' means from its own species. Although people have taken other meanings, the basis for those is very weak. The meaning we have adopted is supported by the Quran itself; in Surah An-Nahl (72), it says: 'And Allah has made for you from yourselves mates.' Obviously, this can only mean that Allah made wives for you from your own species. No one can take this to mean that these wives were created from within each individual.

Meaning of 'Tasa'ul': 'Tasa'ul' means to ask, question, or request from one another. Progressing from this, it is also used in the sense of seeking help from each other. In Surah Al-Mu'minun: 'So when the Horn is blown, there will be no relationship between them that Day, nor will they ask [seek help from] one another.'

Meaning of 'Arham': 'Arham' refers to uterine/kinship relations. By conjoining it with Allah, its importance in religion is clarified. This proves that after God, the first thing deserving of Taqwa and respect is the bond of the womb and its rights. God is the Creator of all, and the womb is the medium and means for everyone's existence; therefore, the rights of God and the womb are mandatory upon everyone. On this basis, Allah Almighty has set the rank of the womb such that whoever joins it, God joins with them, and whoever cuts it, God cuts them off. This is proven by a Hadith Qudsi and also emerges from the Quran.

Fundamental Realities regarding the Organization of Society: The verse under discussion is a comprehensive preamble for all the commands and instructions revealed by Allah Almighty for the organization of human society that follow. The points clarified as fundamental realities in this preamble should be well understood.

First, the Taqwa (piety/consciousness) instructed in this verse has a specific context. This Taqwa implies that this creation did not come into existence by itself but was created by God, who is both the Creator and the Lord of all. Therefore, it is not permissible for anyone to treat it like a masterless, shepherdless stray flock and commit high-handedness or make it a target of oppression; rather, it is everyone's duty to adopt a path of justice and mercy in its affairs, otherwise, remember that God is very Powerful, a great Avenger and Crusher. Those who commit high-handedness in the affairs of His creation will not escape His wrath. He is monitoring everything.

Second, the entire human race is the household of a single Adam. Allah Almighty created everyone from the lineage of the same Adam and Eve. In terms of being the progeny of Adam, all are equal. From this perspective, there is no difference between Arab and non-Arab, red and black, or African and Asian; all are God's creation and all are Adam's children. The bond of God and the womb is shared among all. Its natural demand is that all live as worshippers of the same God and as members of a single shared household, maintaining relationships of right, justice, affection, and love.

Third, just as Adam is the father of the entire human race, Eve is the mother of the entire human race. Allah Almighty created Eve from the same species as Adam; therefore, the woman is not some base, humble, inferior, or naturally sinful creature, but is an equal partner in the honor of humanity. She cannot be deprived of rights by being considered a humble creature, nor can she be made a target of oppression by being thought of as weak.

Fourth, the medium of God and the womb has always been the motive for mutual cooperation and sympathy. Whoever faces a difficulty or danger appeals to others by invoking God and the womb, and since this appeal is based on nature, it is effective in most cases. But those who demand rights in the name of God and the womb often forget that just as it is a right to demand rights on these bases, it is also a duty to fulfill rights. The person who is alert to take in the name of God and the womb but is not ready to give is guilty of deception toward God and disloyalty toward the womb, and this crime can only be committed by one whose heart is empty of the spirit of Taqwa. Those who recognize the rights of God and the womb carry the responsibilities just as they take the benefits from these names, and in reality, this balance between seeking rights and recognizing rights is the original beauty of a true Islamic society. The segment 'And fear Allah, through whom you ask one another, and the wombs' points toward this reality.

Section 2: Subject of Verses 2 to 10:
In the following verses, based on these foundations of Taqwa, justice, and mercy mentioned above, the guardians of orphans are addressed regarding their responsibilities. Guidance is provided for the possible forms of fulfilling this difficult duty while staying within justice—for example, if a person feels they can only monitor an orphan's wealth and rights with full caution if they include the orphan's mother in this responsibility, they can benefit from the permission for polygamy, provided they adhere to the general law of justice, the limit of four, and the payment of dowry (Mahr) for them too. They should not create the excuse that since they married one of them for the benefit of their children, they are therefore free from the responsibilities of justice and Mahr regarding them.

Following this, it is stated when an orphan's wealth should be handed over to them and what path a needy or wealthy guardian should adopt regarding benefiting from that wealth during the period of guardianship.

Then, instruction is given that even after the heirs' rights are determined in the Sharia, if relatives, orphans, and the poor arrive at the time of distributing a deceased person's wealth—even if they have no legal right in it—they should be given something from it and their hearts should be consoled.

Finally, it stated that those who devour the property of orphans through oppression and excess are filling their bellies with fire and will eventually fall into the blazing fire of Hell. In this light, recite the following verses.

'wa atul-yatama amwalahum wa la tatabaddalul-khabitha bit-tayyibi wa la ta'kulu amwalahum ila amwalikum innahu kana huban kabira'.
This verse addresses the guardians of orphans. Its conjunction with the previous verse is proof that the command or prohibition given here is based on those fundamental realities mentioned above.

Meaning of 'Khabith' and 'Tayyib': Just as these words are used for things and beings that are 'vile' or 'pure' from a moral and Shari perspective, they are also used—as passed under verse 267 of Al-Baqarah—for things that are 'defective' or 'fine' in material terms.

The preposition 'ila' (to) with 'akl' (consuming) is proof that 'dhamma' (adding/mixing) or a word of similar meaning is omitted here.

Necessary Instructions for Protecting Orphans' Wealth: Some guardians of orphans, whose breasts are empty of the fear of God, first of all suppress the orphan's entire right; and if they do not suppress it, they create easy administrative opportunities with the intention of embezzlement. They were instructed: give the orphans their wealth. Do not try to digest it yourselves. Then, they were explicitly stopped from the tactics used for this purpose: neither plan to exchange your defective property for their good property, nor try to embezzle their property by mixing it with yours.

If a guardian wants to mix an orphan's wealth with theirs for the sake of administrative ease, although the Sharia has given permission for this—as passed under verse 220 of Surah Al-Baqarah—it is with the condition that the purpose of this mixing and partnership is reform (islah) and not corruption (ifsad); otherwise, the protection of the orphan's right falls upon the Islamic government.

'wa in khiftum alla tuqsitu fil-yatama fankihu ma taba lakum minan-nisa'i mathna wa thulatha wa ruba'a fa-in khiftum alla ta'dilu fawahidatan aw ma malakat aymanukum dhalika adna alla ta'ulu'.
Meaning of 'Yatama': The word 'Yatama' is used for minors whose father has died, regardless of whether they are boys or girls. Its usage for only minor girls is known neither in the Arabic language nor in the Quran and Hadith. This word appears in this plural form in at least fifteen places in the Quran, but nowhere is it used exclusively for orphan girls.

Meaning of 'ma taba lakum': Some interpreters have taken this to mean 'ma halla lakum' (what is lawful for you). This is in accord with the word's usage, although lexically it can also mean "those who are willing/pleased"; the words 'fa-in tibna lakum' in the next verse point toward this. It can also mean those who bring pleasantness to your life. All these meanings fit here, but we prefer the first one because it is most suitable to the context.

'Nisa'' refers to the mothers of the orphans: Although the word 'Nisa'' (women) is apparently general, the context proves that it does not refer to general women but to the mothers of the orphans. Using a general term to mean a specific one, provided a clue exists, is very well-known in Arabic. Examples are abundant in the Quran. Since this clue will become self-evident from the gradual progression of the subject, there is no need to mention its proofs here.

Permission for Polygamy for Expediency: The meaning of the verse is: if you (the addressees are the guardians of orphans) fear out of caution that monitoring the orphans' wealth and their mandatory rights is a difficult task for you—that you cannot fulfill it excellently on your own responsibility—and if the orphans' mother also becomes a partner with you in this responsibility, you can fulfill this duty in an excellent way—because the heart-felt attachment she can have for the orphans cannot be had by anyone else, and the alertness with which she can monitor their rights is not possible for anyone else—then marry those among them who are lawful for you, provided the number of women does not exceed four in any case and you can maintain justice among them. If you fear you cannot maintain justice, then do not marry more than one. It stated: this method is more correct from the viewpoint of keeping you established on truth and justice.

From this, it is known that the condition of justice among wives is such an absolute condition that the Sharia has kept no flexibility in it even for an important religious benefit like monitoring the rights of orphans.

Removal of a Doubt: Some people might face the doubt here: if the interpretation of the verse is this, then it clearly implies that the permission for polygamy in Islam is not absolute but restricted to the benefit of orphans. The answer is that the nature of the issue's description here is not that the permission for polygamy is given *only* with the restriction of orphans' benefit and is otherwise prohibited; rather, it is that from the viewpoint of orphans' benefit, permission is given to benefit from the existing custom of polygamy in Arabia, but it is limited to four. If the intention were to restrict polygamy only to the benefit of orphans, the style of expression would have been entirely different. This style only implies that a restriction was placed on the current form of polygamy to guide toward benefiting from it for a social expediency; but social expediency is not limited only to orphans' benefit; there can be others. Then there is no reason for it to be prohibited.

Answer to another Doubt: Some might have another doubt: we ignored the statement of those who interpreted 'Yatama' as orphan girls merely on the basis that its usage for only girls is not well-known, whereas we interpreted 'Nisa'' as the mothers of orphans when its usage for that meaning is also not well-known. The answer is: we did not ignore that statement only because the lexicon and usage are not in its favor, but also because the interpretation of the verse does not fit correctly with that meaning. If a person feared that if they married an orphan girl, they would fall short in fulfilling her rights because she has no father or brother, then the instruction should have been to postpone marriage with her until she reaches maturity and can understand her rights and duties with her own choice, or simply to marry some other woman; there was no need for the permission of polygamy and the description of its limits and conditions. If it is said that an orphan remains helpless even after maturity due to the absence of father and brother, then the instruction should have been to marry women whose fathers and brothers are alive, because this type of helplessness can afflict other women too even if they haven't faced the helplessness of orphanhood.

It is also worth remembering that if someone has an orphan girl under their care, they train and educate her well, and then marry her with her consent upon her reaching maturity, this is not disliked but liked in the Sharia.

In any case, we left that statement not for one reason but for several, and the specification of the word 'Nisa'' is based on those clues, some of which were mentioned above and some are coming ahead.

'ma malakat aymanukum' refers to slave-girls. Since the condition of justice, etc., is not for them, they were permitted. We wrote on the original nature of this issue in Al-Baqarah. We will discuss it further at a suitable place.

'wa atun-nisa'a saduqatihinna nihlatan fa-in tibna lakum 'an shay'im-minhu nafsan fakuluhu hani'am-mari'a'.
Meaning of 'Nihlah': In this verse too, 'Nisa'' refers to the mothers of the orphans. 'Nahl' means to give something to someone, and when used in relation to a woman, it means to pay the Mahr (dowry). 'Nihlatan' is here for the emphasis of the verb: meaning, give them the Mahr in the way Mahr is given. This emphasis was necessary because when marriage with them is done from the aspect of their children's benefit, a person might think that the restriction of Mahr, etc., should not exist. It stated: no, just as justice is a condition, the payment of Mahr is also a condition, and this Mahr must be paid like a Mahr, not just as a formality.

In 'fa-in tibna lakum 'an shay'im-minhu', the preposition 'an' points toward the concept of 'waiving/foregoing'—meaning if they voluntarily waive any part of their Mahr, you can benefit from it; it can be a wholesome thing for you.

'wa la tu'tus-sufaha'a amwalakumul-lati ja'alallahu lakum qiyaman warzuquhum fiha waksuhum wa qulu lahum qawlam-ma'rufa'.
'Sufaha'' (the immature/foolish) refers to those same orphans under discussion. The meaning is: this command given to you to give orphans their wealth does not mean that if they are entirely ignorant and immature, you should still hand over everything they own to them. Allah has made wealth a means for people's subsistence and survival; therefore, within it, alongside the individual right, there is an aspect of family and collective welfare. From this perspective, its ruin is not just one person's loss but the loss of the entire family and eventually the entire society. This requires that no form be adopted that causes the ruin of any wealth. If the orphan is still ignorant and immature, it is the guardian's duty to keep their wealth under their protection and monitoring, but they should feed them, clothe them, and console them so they are assured that this monitoring is for their own benefit. After becoming capable of handling responsibility, everything of theirs is to be given to them.

In 'warzuquhum fiha', the word 'fiha' (in/within it) yields the hint that guardians should act with generosity in fulfilling the orphans' needs. They should not adopt the attitude of stingy and miserly guardians. In Arabic, when one says 'arzuquhum fiha', it means feed and clothe them with abundance; and if one says 'arzuquhum minha' (as in the next verse), it means give them something from it.

'wabtalul-yatama hatta idha balaghun-nikaha fa-in anastum-minhum rushdan fadfa'u ilayhim amwalahum wa la ta'kuluhu israfaw-wa bidaran ay-yakbaru wa man kana ghaniyyan falyasta'fif wa man kana faqiran fal-ya'kul bil-ma'rufi fa-idha dafa'tum ilayhim amwalahum fa-ash-hidu 'alayhim wa kafa billahi hasiba'.
When should orphans' wealth be handed over to them? This describes the method guardians should adopt in handing over the orphans' wealth. It stated: test the orphans—meaning keep testing their capability by entrusting some small responsibility to them to see if the understanding of transactions is being produced within them or not. This treatment should be maintained until the age of marriage, i.e., maturity. When they reach maturity, if it is felt that the capability to carry their responsibilities has been produced within them, then their wealth should be handed over to them.

Sexual Maturity does not necessitate Rational Maturity: There is a clear hint in the verse that sexual maturity does not necessitate rational maturity in every case. There can be many who reach maturity physically but remain immature in mind. One should not be in a hurry regarding such immature adults. However, it is certain that this should not be made a pretext for remaining in control of their wealth; rather, whatever is done should be done with their welfare in view.

Permission for a Poor Guardian to benefit from the Orphan's wealth according to 'Ma'ruf': If the guardian is a person of means, they should abstain from taking anything from the orphan's wealth. If they are poor, they can benefit from it according to custom (Ma'ruf). 'According to custom' means taking a benefit that is within the limits of reasonableness in terms of the nature of responsibilities, the status of the property, local conditions, and the guardian's standard of living; it should not be of a nature that every reasonable person gets the impression that an attempt was made to digest the orphan's property through extravagance and haste out of fear of the orphan reaching maturity.

Instruction to appoint Witnesses at the time of Handing Over Wealth: Finally, the instruction was given: when you are about to hand over the orphan's wealth to them, appoint some reliable and trustworthy people as witnesses so that no possibility of bad suspicion or dispute remains. And along with this, remember that the account of all transactions must also be given to God. If any kind of betrayal occurred, it is possible that the eyes of the world's witnesses and observers did not fall upon it, but God's eye can never miss anything.

The Protection of Orphans' Rights opened the path for others' rights: After the protection of orphans' rights, this is now the preamble to the law of inheritance in which no opportunity remains for men and agnate relatives to gather and occupy all the deceased's assets and property. Before Islam, not only in Arabia but in the entire world, the situation was such that let alone orphans and women, all weak heirs were at the mercy of powerful heirs. The Quran pointed to this situation elsewhere with the words: 'And you consume inheritance, devouring [it] altogether.' To end this situation, the Quran determined the rights of all heirs—men and women alike. A person reciting the previous verses feels upon reaching this verse that, through the blessing of the orphans, the path to determining others' rights also opened. Meaning: those who were themselves deprived of rights not only obtained rights but, because of them, others also obtained rights. Specifically, the mention of women appeared in a way as if for the first time they too obtained a place in the ranks of rightful claimants alongside men, and a specific portion from the legacy of their parents and relatives—whether small or large—was made mandatory for them by Allah Almighty.

After the portions are determined, the legal rightful claimants will only be those who are designated as heirs by the Sharia; however, the general rights of 'Silah-e-Rahm' (joining ties) and family and human sympathy will still remain. Consequently, addressing the heirs, the instruction was given: if relatives, orphans, and the poor are present at the time of distributing someone's inheritance, then although they have no Shari right in the inheritance, they should nonetheless not be rebuked but an attempt should be made to console them by giving them something from it.

It stated: this point should not be forgotten—that just as others' children became orphans, their own children could also have become orphans.

Then let them think: if they were leaving orphans behind, what kind of apprehensions would they have in their hearts regarding them? For this reason, they should fear Allah and speak straight words.

Finally, the final warning was given: those who are filling their bellies with the orphans' wealth through oppression and deprivation of rights are, in terms of the end result, filling their bellies with fire, and in the Hereafter, they will fall into the blazing fire of Hell carrying this fire.

Subject of Verses 11 to 14:
Further on, according to those demands of Taqwa, justice, and mercy upon which the foundation of Islamic society is established, the clarification of that Shari distribution of inheritance was made to which the seventh verse hinted, so that a very large cause of oppression, deprivation of rights, dispute, and difference is ended.

Exegesis of Verses 11 to 14: The commands of inheritance described in this group of verses are clear in themselves and their details are also present in the books of 'Fara'id' (inheritance law); therefore, we will suffice with clarifying only some important points.

The Correct Meaning of 'Wasiyyah': The first noteworthy thing is that here Allah Almighty has described the commands regarding the distribution of inheritance as His 'will' (Wasiyyah). The correct meaning of 'Wasiyyah' in the Arabic language is for someone to place a responsibility on another that when such-and-such a situation arises, they should adopt such-and-step method or conduct. It contains the aspect of the testator's foresight, well-wishing, and compassion, and within it is also found the responsibility of a covenant and treaty. I did not find any word in Urdu to express all these implications of the word. The word I have chosen [Will/Instruction] does not fully encompass its meaning.

The Reason for the Male's Share being Double that of the Female: The second thing is that Allah Almighty has kept the male's share in inheritance double that of the female. The reason is that in Islam's social system, Allah Almighty has placed all maintenance responsibilities upon the man. No responsibility is placed upon the woman. The man alone is made responsible for the wife's maintenance and the children's guardianship. The Quran has also clearly stated that by his creative attributes, the man is the one capable of being made the 'Qawwam' (maintainer) and head of the family, and this 'Qawwam-ship' is indispensable for the family's order and its survival; if the family has no maintainer, it is against the family's nature, and if the family's maintainer is the woman instead of the man, it is against human nature, and every opposition to nature will necessarily be a cause of corruption and disorder which will ruin the entire society. This required that the man have preference in some rights in view of his responsibilities. Those who claim perfect equality of man and woman in every aspect—their claim is entirely contrary to reason and nature. We will discuss this issue further in this Surah, and we have also written an independent book on this in which all aspects of this issue are discussed, titled 'The Status of Woman in Islamic Society.'

Violation of God's Distribution is Contempt for His Wisdom: The third thing is that the Wise Quran has warned that this distribution is based on Allah Almighty's knowledge and His wisdom. Allah Almighty's knowledge encompasses the front and the back, and the manifest and the hidden. No one's knowledge can encompass His knowledge. Similarly, in His every word and every work, there is extremely deep wisdom, and no one holds the rank to understand all the nuances of His wisdom. For this reason, no one should object to this distribution of God in the pride of their own knowledge and philosophy, nor should anyone take any step against it in the zeal of emotional partiality. Sometimes a person gives preference to one over another based on their personal inclination, but this preference is wrong from both worldly and afterlife perspectives; similarly, they ignore someone based on personal inclination, even though later circumstances prove that the way of the one they ignored was more correct from both worldly and afterlife perspectives. Thus, the correct method is that a person takes every step according to the Sharia's instruction instead of their personal inclinations. In this is goodness and blessing. Those who take steps against the Sharia show contempt for God's knowledge and wisdom, the punishment for which they generally receive in the world too, and in the Hereafter it is mandatory anyway. Reflect on 'la tadruna ayyuhum aqrabu lakum naf'a' (you do not know which of them is nearer to you in benefit) in this light.

A Will in favor of Heirs is not Permissible: The fourth thing is that when God has described this distribution as His 'will', its meaning is that for those whom He designated as heirs of a testator, He has personally made a will based on justice and wisdom. After this will of the Kind and Wise Lord, if a testator makes a will for an heir, it is in reality a 'correction'—or in more correct words, an opposition—to God's will, which is entirely contrary to Taqwa. From this, the point emerges clearly that the permission for a will given to testators does not relate to those heirs regarding whom God's own will exists, but is specific to non-heirs. Consequently, on this basis is the Prophet's (PBUH) statement: 'la wasiyyata li-warith' (no will for an heir).

The Wisdom of the Qualifier 'ghayra mudharr': Fifth, the emphasis on fulfilling the testator's will and paying their debt which appears repeatedly is accompanied by the condition 'ghayra mudharr' (without causing harm). Although this condition is mentioned only in the context of 'Kalalah' (one leaving no parents or children), the context proves it is intended everywhere. Its mention with 'Kalalah' is only because a testator who has neither roots nor branches has a great possibility of the desire arising within them not to let their property transfer to those people toward whom they have no natural inclination, even though they are the legal rightful claimants. For this, they might exceed in the will or display a false show of debt. To stop this tendency, the Quran placed the condition on both the will and the debt that it be "ghayra mudharr"—meaning its purpose is not merely to harm the Shari heirs. On this basis, the Holy Prophet (PBUH) limited the will to one-third of the wealth so that the original heirs' rights are not deprived by it.

Subject of Verses 15 to 18:
Temporary Command for Restraining Sexual Anarchy: In the previous verses, the doors of those corruptions were closed which arise from the excessive greed for wealth and cause disorder in society and the cutting of ties. Now, further on, restrictions are being imposed on sexual anarchy and lustful lack of restraint, because this lack of restraint too, like the greed for wealth—rather, even more than it—turns society into a playground for Satan.

But it should be remembered that these commands are the initial commands of this chapter, relating to that period when the Islamic society in Madinah was not yet fully organized and stable. Non-Muslim tribes existed around Madinah who had not yet come under the sway of Islam, and the Muslims had not yet achieved that power to implement Islamic limits and penalties upon them too. This situation was a complex one. It was neither wise to implement the limits and penalties necessary for the purification of society without hesitation—because the opponents could take wrong advantage of it—nor was it possible to leave the doors of indecency and the 'Munkar' open, because that would have fanned that tendency of immorality and indecency which was strong in Arab society at that time and which Islam had come to erase. Keeping both these aspects in view, Islam adopted a wise method: as far as the society of Muslims was concerned, to keep it pure from immorality and indecency, it gave some such initial temporary commands which were both useful for blocking corruptions and were preparers of the Muslims' minds for accepting the commands that were revealed later in this series; and simultaneously, they contained the aspect that the opponents could not make them a means for whispers and machinations against Islam. In this light, recite the following verses.

'Fahishah' is well-known for denoting Adultery: 'Fahishah' refers to open indecency and immorality, and this word is well-known for denoting adultery (Zina).

Meaning of 'min nisa'ikum': 'min nisa'ikum' (from your women), meaning the woman committing the immorality belongs to the society of Muslims.

Hint that this Command is Temporary: In 'aw yaj'alallahu lahunna sabila' (or Allah ordains for them a way), there is a hint that this command is temporary. The final command in this chapter was to be revealed later. Consequently, this promise was fulfilled by the punishment for Zina described in Surah An-Nur.

Use of the Form based on the Dominant Partner: 'wal-ladhani ya'tiyaniha minkum' (And the two who commit it among you)—meaning both parties committing the immorality, the man and the woman, belong to the Muslims; in this, the masculine form is used according to the well-known rule of the Arabic language based on the dominant partner. Like the word "Walidayn" (parents), which is masculine but used for both mother and father.

Meaning of 'Idha'': 'fa-adhuhuma' (punish them both) includes humiliation and belittling, rebuke and reprimand, and beating to the extent of reform.

In these verses, the address is obviously to the 'Arbab-e-Hall o 'Aqd' (those in authority) and responsible persons of the society. Addressing them, two separate instructions were given for the penalty of immorality in two different situations.

Two separate instructions for two situations: One situation is that the woman committing the immorality belongs to the society of Muslims but her male partner is not under the pressure of the Islamic society. In such a situation, the instruction was given to confine the woman inside the house, placing a full restriction on her coming and going until death ends her or a new command is revealed by Allah Almighty in this chapter. The second situation is that both parties of the immorality belong to the Muslims. In such a situation, an attempt should be made to correct them through rebuke and reprimand, humiliation and belittling, and beating to the extent of reform; if they repent and correct their conduct due to its effect, then they should be overlooked. Allah is Accepting of repentance and Merciful.

The Wisdom of the Intensity of Caution regarding the Woman: Reflecting on these two situations reveals that in the first situation, the aspect of caution is observed with more intensity. In the second situation, both the woman and the man were given the opportunity to be overlooked if they repent and correct their conduct; but in the first situation, it was not stated regarding the woman that if she repents and reforms, the restriction imposed upon her be lifted. The reason for this apparently seems to be that in the second situation, both parties are under the pressure of the Islamic society, any change in their attitude will be before everyone, and their influences and resources are known and determined; for them, any action independent of their family and tribe will be, if not impossible, then extremely difficult. But in the first situation, the man—who holds the status of the dominant partner in the original crime—is entirely free from the pressure of the Islamic society; nothing is known of his attitude, nothing estimated of his intentions, and the limits of his influences and resources are not known or determined. In such a state, if the woman were given the opportunity to be overlooked after repentance, this point could have produced extremely dangerous results. First, without considering the man's attitude, a correct estimate of the woman's repentance and reform is not even possible; and even if it were, when the man is entirely out of control and autonomous, the possibilities of abduction, escape, and murder cannot be ignored in any state; from this perspective, the intensity of caution is respected in it.

Although these penalties were abrogated after the limits (Hudud) revealed in Surah An-Nur, this code of testimony regarding immorality remained afterward too.

Justification for the Prison System for Penal Purposes: Furthermore, from the words 'fa-amsikuhunna fil-buyut' (confine them in the houses) in the previous verse, the justification for the prison system for penal purposes also emerges.

Meaning of the word 'Jahalat': 'Jahalat' in Arabic does not only mean 'not knowing,' but its dominant usage is for the meaning of committing some mischief, oppression, or sin while being overcome by emotions. This word is generally used as the opposite of 'Hilm' (forbearance) rather than 'Ilm' (knowledge). A Hamasa poet says: 
'wa lil-hilmi khayrun fa'laman maghabbatan... minal-jahli illa an tushammasa min zhulmi' 
(And remember that forbearance is better in its end result than ignorance/passion, unless you are provoked by oppression).
A famous verse of the Mu'allaqat: 
'ala la yajhalan ahadun 'alayna... fa-najhala fawqa jahli-l-jahilina' 
(Let no one act passionately/ignorantly against us, lest we act even more passionately than all the passionate ones).

Conditions for the Acceptance of Repentance: In the previous verse, the statement "if they repent and reform, then overlook them" made it perfectly clear that the reform of conduct is among the mandatory conditions of repentance; if a person does not cease the evil they were committing, then no matter how much they chant "repentance, repentance" with the tongue, their repentance is entirely unauthorized. In this connection, the etiquettes and characteristics of repentance were further clarified. It stated: the right of repentance upon Allah is established only for those who commit some evil while overcome by emotions and then repent immediately. It is the repentance of these people that Allah accepts. Allah Almighty is Knowing and Wise. He is neither unaware of any matter nor is any of His works devoid of wisdom. Why then would He take any responsibility for the repentance of those who knowingly and with a cool heart continue to commit sins while also reciting the litany of repentance?

Similarly, the repentance of those is not repentance who remained drowned in sins throughout their lives, and when they saw death standing over their heads, they said: "Now I repent!" By the same analogy, the repentance of those who die in the state of Kufr is also not [accepted].

A Question and its Answer: Reflecting on these two verses (17 and 18) determines two forms of the acceptance and non-acceptance of repentance. For those who commit some evil while overcome by emotions and then immediately repent and reform, Allah Almighty has taken the responsibility to accept their repentance upon Himself. In contrast, those who continue to commit sins until the Angel of Death pounces upon them, at which time they repent, or those who die in the state of Kufr—their repentance is not accepted. After these two limits are determined, one question remains: what is the ruling for the repentance of those who were not blessed with the success of repenting immediately after the sin, but they also did not delay so much that the time of death arrived? Regarding the answer to this question, this verse is silent, and this silence creates hope just as it creates fear, and the intent of the Wise Quran seems to be that this matter remains 'between hope and fear' (bayn al-raja' wal-khawf). But sometimes the mind goes toward the point that such people of this Ummah will, hopefully, attain salvation through the intercession of the Prophet (PBUH) because no reason exists for intercession being prohibited in their case.

Subject of Verses 19 to 22: Further instructions in the same series as the instructions given above for securing women's rights within society and protecting them from oppression and excess.

'ya-ayyu-hal-ladhina amanu la yahillu lakum an tarithu-n-nisa'a karhan wa la ta'dhuluhunna litadh-habu bi-ba'dhi ma ataytumuhunna illa ay-ya'tina bi-fahishatim-mubayyinah wa 'ashiruhunna bil-ma'rufi fa-in karihtumuhunna fa-'asa ay-takrahu shay'aw-wa yaj'alallahu fihi khayran kathira'.
'wa la ta'dhuluhunna'. Meaning of 'Adhl': "'Adhl" means to distress, to corner, and to obstruct.

Living according to 'Ma'ruf': 'wa 'ashiruhunna bil-ma'ruf', meaning deal with them in a way that is befitting of the noble, according to reason and nature, and based on mercy, chivalry, and justice. Through the use of the word 'Ma'ruf' here, the point emerges that although some very improper types of excesses had become prevalent regarding the treatment of women in some classes of the pagan Arabs, they nonetheless were not unacquainted with what reasonable treatment of a woman is.

Reform of a Loathsome Custom of the pagan Arabs: In this verse, first a very loathsome custom of the pagan Arabs was reformed. It was that in some of their classes, the custom existed that like the deceased's property and livestock, his wives were also transferred to the heir. The limit was that sons even occupied the wedded wives of their fathers. Upon the father's death, the eldest successor would throw his cloak over whichever of his [father's] wives he wished, and they would all come into his disposal, and from verse 22 ahead it is clear that they did not even feel any repulsion in establishing marital relations with them. The Quran clarified here that the woman is not a left-behind property but a free being. Dealing with her like the deceased's sheep and goats is not permissible; rather, she is the owner of her own will and free within the limits of the Sharia.

Kind Treatment of a Disliked Wife: Second, it stated that if someone dislikes his wife, he should not try to put her in distress and corner her to make her vomit out what he gave her or spent on her. This type of attitude is permissible only in the form when open immorality is issued from her. If no such thing has been issued from her and she remains firm on her loyalty and chastity, then merely on the basis that the wife is not liked, to distress her to extort something from her is entirely contrary to reason, justice, nobility, and chivalry. The only thing worthy of hatred is moral corruption. Merely on the basis of disliking the shape and form or color and complexion, it is not permissible that she be deprived of the rights of noble living. It is possible that a person dislikes his wife merely on the basis of shape and form, but Allah Almighty opens many doors of blessings in both his world and the Hereafter through her. Thus, the correct believer-like attitude is that if someone faces this type of trial, they should deal with such a wife extremely well despite the lack of suitability in taste, in view of the fear of God and their own chivalry and nobility, and hope for goodness and blessing from God.

A Literary Point: Although the word "'asa" (perhaps) is used here—which in Arabic comes only for expressing hope and expectation—those who recognize the nuances of Arabic know that in such instances, as is the case here, a kind of promise from Allah Almighty is hidden. The reality glimpsed behind this hint is that for those who give importance to high moral and human values over outward shape and form and sacrifice their emotions for their sake, there is a promise of 'abundant good' (khayr-e-kathir) from Allah Almighty. Those who have played the gamble for this promise bear witness that this point is a hundred percent truth, and whose word can be truer than God's?

Exegesis of Verses 21 and 22:
'wa in aradtumustibdala zawjim-makana zawjiw-wa ataytum ihdahunna qintaran fala ta'khudhu minhu shay'a. a-ta'khudhunahu buhtanaw-wa ithmam-mubina. wa kayfa ta'khudhunahu wa qad afdha ba'dhukum ila ba'dhiw-wa akhadhna minkum mithaqan ghalizha'.
Meaning of 'Qintar': 'Qintar' is originally a weight whose amount has varied with time, but in general usage, it refers to a vast amount of wealth. Just as we say "tons of wealth" or "heaps of wealth," this word is for the expression of that meaning in Arabic. From this, the construction 'qanatira muqantarah' is also used in the Quran.

Meaning of 'afdha ba'dhukum ila ba'dh': 'afdha falanun ila falan' means he reached him and entered his sphere. Thus, 'afdha ila falanin bi-sirrihi' means he unveiled all his secrets before him. This is an extremely comprehensive and extremely sophisticated expression for the marital relations of husband and wife. Both become unveiled for each other in such a way that no corner and no aspect of their outward and inward and feelings and emotions remains hidden from each other.

Taking back wealth given to the Wife is contrary to Chivalry: In the previous verse, it was told that despite [dislike], the higher method is for a person to try to maintain with the wife in a sophisticated way. Now it is being told that if a person, due to the demands of their circumstances, has reached the decision to leave one wife and marry another woman, then they should nonetheless not do this: to try to take back what they gave the first wife. Even if they gave her heaps of wealth, it is still not permissible for them to use tactics to take it back. Especially, slandering her with this thought—to create a justification for taking back the given wealth—is an even greater sin and oppression. Then it stated: it is entirely contrary to a man's chivalry that regarding the woman with whom he bound a lifelong pledge of loyalty, who came into his bond of marriage under an extremely firm covenant, who unveiled her entire outward and inward for him, and with whom he spent a duration of life as 'one soul in two bodies'—when the turn for separation comes, an attempt is made to make her vomit out what he fed and clothed her, to the extent that she is even made a target of slanders and accusations for this base purpose.

The Marriage Covenant is a Firm Treaty: There is another thing worth considering here: it stated 'wa akhadhna minkum mithaqan ghalizha' (and they have taken from you a firm covenant). Obviously, "firm covenant" (mithaq ghalizh) here refers to the marriage contract ('aqd-e-nikah). There is no clue for any other covenant here, nor any historical testimony. Then the question arises: why was the responsibility of the marriage contract described here as a "firm covenant"? In my view, the reason is that the original customary and Shari reality of the marriage contract is that it is a firm treaty of rights and responsibilities between husband and wife, through which both join each other with the resolve of lifelong union, and both equally obtain rights and equally carry responsibilities for each other. Apparently, the words of this covenant are extremely simple and brief, but its implications and corollaries are many, and these implications and corollaries are known and recognized in every civilized society and every Sharia. It is also a reality that this covenant is bound between husband and wife, but the knot in it is tied by God's command, and just as the creation is its witness, the Creator too is its witness. Then what doubt remained in its being a firm covenant? By describing this relationship with this word here, the Quran has clarified its original greatness: that the man should not forget in any state that his connection with the wife is not bound by a weak thread, but this relationship is an extremely firm relationship, and under it, just as there are rights for the man, there are also rights for the wife from which there is no room for the man to flee. If he tries to run from them, he will disgrace his own chivalry and also displease his God.

'wa la tankihu ma nakaha aba'ukum minan-nisa'i illa ma qad salaf innahu kana fahishataw-wa maqtaw-wa sa'a sabila'.
'Maqt' and 'Mamqut' refer to an odious and hateful thing or act. For marriage with a father's wedded wife, the expression 'zawaj al-maqt' (marriage of hatred) was famous. Similarly, the person who committed this heinous act was called 'Maqti'.

'illa ma qad salaf' (except what has already passed) means that this law will not apply to the past—that it be made the basis for investigating all previous relationships and issuing rulings of permission and prohibition in their light. This thing is practically impossible. Law is by its nature something whose implementation is upon the present and future. Consequently, by overlooking the past, those evils were reformed which were actually present, and this indecency was blocked for the future.

'Zawaj al-Maqt': It passed under verse 19 that in some classes of the pagan Arabs, the custom existed that the father's wedded wives were inherited by the son, and the sons did not feel any repulsion in establishing marital relations with them. This verse gave a final prohibition of this heinous act. It stated: this act is open indecency and immorality, extremely odious, and an extremely bad custom.

Mentioning the Evils of Specific Classes with a General Form: It is worth remembering that the mention of such evils and indecencies in the Quran with a general form does not mean that the entire nation was necessarily afflicted with it. Sometimes it happens that an evil is limited within a specific class, but the law related to it addresses everyone, so the address is general. The words used here for this evil are themselves witnesses that its being open indecency and odious was known to the noble Arabs too.

Section 10: Subject of Verses 23 to 25:
The prohibition of marriage with the father's wedded wives cleared the way for describing the permissions and prohibitions among women, so that the pollutions and injustices in society from this aspect become clear and can be reformed. It stated:

'hurrimat 'alaykum ummahatukum wa banatukum wa akhawatukum wa 'ammatukum wa khalatukum wa banatul-akhi wa banatul-ukhti wa ummahatukumul-lati ardha'nakum wa akhawatukum-minar-radha'ati wa ummahatu nisa'ikum wa raba'ibukumul-lati fi hujurikum-min nisa'ikumul-lati dakhaltum bihinna fa-il-lam takunu dakhaltum bihinna fala junaha 'alaykum wa hala'ilu abna'ikumul-ladhina min ashlabikum wa an tajma'u baynal-ukhtayni illa ma qad salaf innallaha kana ghafurar-rahima'.

The prohibitions described in this verse are based on that demand of human nature: where the proximity of a uterine/kinship relation exists or a similarity to it is found, the foundation of mutual connection should be only on the high emotions of mercy, love, and compassion; no mixture of the soul's lusts and desires should exist in it, nor should the opportunity be given for jealousy and rivalry to interfere. This is contrary to the original nature (fitrah) upon which the Creator of the universe created the human. For this reason, Allah Almighty has prohibited marital relations with all those women who possess direct or indirect uterine proximity.

The Relationship of Breastfeeding (Radha'ah) possesses a similarity to the Maternal Relationship: People among us do not take the connection of breastfeeding in that deep sense in which people in Arabia took it. The reason is merely the difference in custom. Otherwise, the reality is that it has a very deep suitability with the maternal relationship. The child who is nurtured in the lap of a mother, from the milk of her breasts, becomes her half-mother if not full. Then how is it possible that their emotions and feelings are not influenced by the one whose milk is running through their veins? If they are not influenced, this is not the order of nature but its corruption, and it was necessary for Islam, which is the religion of nature, to correct this corruption.

The Relationship of Breastfeeding has delivered Great Benefits to Society: The correct estimation of the benefits society has received by giving the connection of breastfeeding its correct place is also generally not made; otherwise, the reality is that this relationship turned the mothers and sisters of beggars and shepherds into the mothers and sisters of leaders—rather, of sovereigns. By the blessing of this relationship, such links were established between villagers and city-dwellers, and between the poor and the wealthy, that nothing could break them.

But it is certain that this connection is not established merely by some accidental incident. From the words in which the Quran has described it here, the point emerges clearly that it is authoritative only when it has come into action with care and as an objective, not accidentally. First, He stated: "your mothers who suckled you." Then He used the word 'Radha'ah' for it. 'akhawatukum-minar-radha'ah'. Those with knowledge of the Arabic language know that 'irdha'' is from the 'If'al' pattern, in which there is effectively a sense of 'doing with intent/intensity.' Similarly, the word 'Radha'ah' also refuses the idea that if a woman puts her breast to the mouth of a crying child merely to soothe them, this would be called 'Radha'ah'.

In what form is the Step-daughter (Rabibah) prohibited? 'Rabibah' refers to the wife's daughter from her previous husband. Since she acquires a similarity to one's own daughter, she too was declared prohibited. In describing the prohibition of these girls, they were mentioned with two attributes. One, that they have been raised in your lap of training; second, that they are from the womb of your consummated wife. Both these attributes were mentioned to make the ruling of prohibition effective. In the Arabic language, every attribute does not necessarily acquire the status of a condition such that if one is not found, the ruling becomes void; rather, it depends on the context. The context tells which attribute holds the rank of a condition and which attribute is merely for painting the picture of the situation. Here, there is not just a context but an explicit statement: if the Rabibah's mother has not become your مدخولہ (consummated wife), there is no evil in marrying that Rabibah. This clarified that the original effective thing in the Rabibah's prohibition is her mother being consummated. If she is consummated, marriage with her daughter will be unlawful, regardless of whether she was raised in the lap of training or not. It should be remembered that in high Arabic—especially in the Wise Quran—points described in the style of negation after affirmation or affirmation after negation are not merely rhetorical but for a specific benefit. In most cases, their objective is the removal of ambiguity. For this reason, the thought of those who consider marriage with a Rabibah prohibited only when she was raised in the trainer's lap is contrary to the Quran. Otherwise, they consider marriage with her permissible.

The Cause for Prohibiting the Joining of Two Sisters (Jam' baynal-ukhtayn): The prohibition of joining two sisters is also based on that same principle of wisdom to which we hinted above: that the Wise Quran wants to highlight that demand of human nature where the natural foundation of mutual connection should be mercy and compassion. This required that those causes be suppressed which would dissolve the poison of jealousy and rivalry within uterine relations. Since in the form of two sisters being simultaneously in someone's bond of marriage, there is a dominant possibility that the two sisters—despite being sisters—fall into the emotions of co-wife burning and jealousy and rivalry, therefore its door was closed. Since this same situation existed in the form of joining an aunt and her niece, the Prophet (PBUH)—as clear from Hadiths—prohibited joining them too.

The difference the Quran made between the wives of biological sons and adopted sons will be discussed in detail at the suitable place in Surah Al-Ahzab. Here, just remember that the qualifier 'min ashlabikum' (from your loins) with 'sons' excluded the wives of adopted sons from this ruling.

'wal-muhsanatu minan-nisa'i illa ma malakat aymanukum kitaballahi 'alaykum wa uhilla lakum ma wara'a dhalikum an tabtaghu bi-amwalikum muhsinina ghayra musafihin famastamta'tum bihi minhunna fa-atuhunna ujurahunna faridhatan wa la junaha 'alaykum fima taradhaytum bihi mim-ba'dil-faridhati innallaha kana 'aliman hakima'.

Meaning of 'Ihsan': 'Ihsan' means both taking something into one's protection and support, and being in someone's protection and support. From this comes the word 'Muhsanat', which is spoken for women who are in the bond of marriage. Also, it is used as the opposite word to slave-girls. In this form, it applies to free women and noble-born women. The Holy Quran has used it in both these meanings. In this verse, in the first meaning; in the next verse, in the second meaning.

Meaning of 'Safh' and 'Musafahat': 'Safh' literally means to pour. From this comes 'Musafahat', which means luxury and immorality, because in it too both the woman and the man make the mere enjoyment of pleasure the objective and waste their seminal fluid. The meaning is: in the list of the aforementioned prohibitions, those women are also included who are in the bond of marriage, because no woman can be in the wedlock of two men simultaneously. Only 'milk yamin' (what the right hand possesses) is exempt from this; her coming into someone's ownership itself renders her previous marriage—which occurred in the 'Abode of War' (Dar al-Harb)—as void.

Two Fundamental Conditions for Marriage: Apart from these women, marriage with other women is permissible, but with two conditions, and both these conditions are required simultaneously. One, that the marriage be with wealth, i.e., Mahr. Second, that the objective of this marriage be to take the woman into one's support and protection, and not to merely satisfy lust temporarily by giving vent to the soul's agitation.

The Original Purpose of the Condition of Mahr: By placing the condition of wealth and Mahr, one objective is to completely block the possibilities of coercion or inheritance regarding women, to which hints passed above. Although much of this was also blocked by declaring uterine relations prohibited, this condition made it even more closed. The second objective is that the matter of marriage acquires the status of a serious treaty and cannot be made child's play. A transaction accompanied by the condition of paying wealth—and the status of this payment is not merely a donation or favor but a duty, to the extent that even if not mentioned, it is necessarily understood as implicit and its payment becomes mandatory based on the woman's customary status—becomes an important and serious matter in both Sharia and custom. No person of reason would dare to become a party in such a treaty until they have thought a hundred times and prepared themselves for carrying the responsibilities of participation in it. Due to these benefits, the condition of Mahr became necessary. Those whose gaze has not gone toward these benefits think that this condition has degraded the woman to the level of a buyable and sellable object. This thought is merely the result of lack of understanding. This condition is a warning: whoever wants to step into the sanctuary (Haram) of a woman should step after thinking well. There is no room for a joke in the matter of marriage and divorce. Here, even a joke holds the status of reality. (Hush-dar ke rah bar dam-e-tegh ast qadam ra).

The Condition of 'Ihsan' is the Negation of 'Mut'ah': The condition of 'Ihsan' was placed to distinguish marriage from 'Sifah' (immorality). The original purpose of marriage is fulfilled only when 'Ihsan' is found with it. Meaning: a man takes a woman into his support and protection with a serious intention and the resolve of a lifelong union, and the woman enters his fortress of support with that same awareness and intention. Without this 'Ihsan', that purpose which nature wanted to fulfill from the relationship of woman and man cannot be fulfilled. If someone establishes a temporary and provisional connection with a woman, then even if they fulfilled the ritual of marriage for it and gave her wealth, this is not 'Ihsan'. This is merely searching for a urinal to urinate, the purpose of which is merely to temporarily lighten the bladder's burden. The Quran, by placing this condition, ended forever that loathsome custom of 'Mut'ah' which was prevalent in the era of ignorance. 

Further on, it stated that the determined Mahr be paid as a duty. However, if after determining the Mahr, the husband and wife make some increase or decrease in it through mutual consent, there is no harm in it. Finally, the reference to the attributes of 'Alim' and 'Hakim' is for the expression of this law's greatness and wisdom: that the One who revealed this law is Knowing and Wise; therefore, His every word is based on infallible knowledge and bottomless wisdom. For others, it is neither permissible to violate it, nor is it permissible to try to amend or reform it.

'wa man lam yastati' minkum tawlan ay-yankihal-muhsanatil-mu'minati fa-mim-ma malakat aymanukum-min fatayatikumul-mu'minati wallahu a'lamu bi-imanikum ba'dhukum-mim-ba'd fankihuhunna bi-idhni ahlihinna wa atuhunna ujurahunna bil-ma'rufi muhsanatin ghayra musafihatiw-wa la muttakhidhati akhdan fa-idha uhsinna fa-in atayna bi-fahishatin fa-'alayhinna nishfu ma 'alal-muhsanati minal-'adhab dhalika liman khashiyal-'anata minkum wa an tashbiru khayrul-lakum wallahu ghafurur-rahim'.

'Tawl' means power, wealth, and favor.

'wallahu a'lamu bi-imanikum': meaning the original foundation of honor and nobility is on faith. It is possible that a woman, while being a slave-girl, surpasses great noble-born men and women in terms of her faith. For this reason, merely on the basis of the thought that a woman is a slave-girl, one should not ignore this aspect of the possibility of honor within her. And faith is such a thing that the true scale for measuring and weighing it is with Allah alone; others cannot correctly estimate it.

'ba'dhukum-mim-ba'd' (you are of one another) contains a hint that being a slave-girl or slave is a temporary state. In terms of lineage, all slaves and free, and all ladies and handmaidens, are from the lineage of the same Adam and the same Eve. 

Indefiniteness of the word 'Fahishah': 'Fahishah' refers to adultery. Its indefiniteness (tankir) is for expressing loathing and hatred, just as 'natmisa wujuha' (We wipe out faces) appeared in verse 47 of this Surah; we will clarify that indefiniteness there. 'Anat' means trouble and hardship, but its usage is for those troubles and hardships that become a cause of trial and a place of slipping for a person.

Permission for Marriage with others' Slave-girls with Caution: The aim of the verse is: if someone does not possess the power to marry a noble-born woman, they should marry a Muslim slave-girl. The original foundation of honor and nobility is faith, and the state of faith is known only to Allah. As far as species and lineage are concerned, all are one in this regard because all are the children of the same Adam and Eve. So, there is no harm in marriage with a slave-girl; however, the condition is that this marriage be with their owners' permission and these slave-girls be given Mahr according to custom. Furthermore, these slave-girls too should live bound by the restriction of 'Ihsan' (chastity/marriage); mere temporary enjoyment and 'friendship-acquaintance' should not be the objective. After coming into this restriction of 'Ihsan', if they commit adultery, they will be given half of the punishment described in the Surah for noble-born women—i.e., fifty lashes. 

Finally, it stated that this permission to marry slave-girls is for those who fear that if they do not marry somewhere, they will fall into sin. For those who can keep themselves in control, patience is better.

The Status of Slave-girls and Slaves in Islamic Society: We have expressed this thought in several places before: that slaves and slave-girls are not a part of Islam's own social system; rather, this thing was already present as a solution to the problem of prisoners of war and the international circumstances of that time, which Islam tolerated. If Islam had ended it unilaterally within its sphere, it would have risked spreading extremely severe chaos within the Muslim society, and enemy nations could also have taken a wrong type of advantage from it. To end it completely, it was necessary that the consciousness of human equality first be awakened among people at the international level. Consequently, Islam placed such rules and regulations in its own system that would both awaken the consciousness of human equality regarding this lowly class and gradually make it so high in mental and moral terms that it would obtain its correct place in the Islamic society.

Some Rulings for Elevating the Status of Slaves and Slave-girls: We have written while discussing the issues of 'Mukatab' (contracted slave) and 'Umm al-Walad' (mother of the master's child), etc., that in this way Islam opened an extremely wide path for the freedom of all capable slaves and slave-girls. Now, reflecting on this verse reveals that in it, attention was drawn toward extremely important realities for elevating this lowly class.

First, Muslims were reminded that the foundation of honor and nobility is Islam, and this is something that a slave can be blessed with just as a free person can. As for the matter of lineage and descent, in this regard, free and slave—rather, all humans—are equal. Then why should these be considered humble?

Second, the same conditions of Mahr and 'Ihsan' were appointed for slave-girls as were for free women so that their standard in society would be elevated. In the case of committing adultery, a punishment was also appointed for them so that gradually their moral standard would come to the society's standard. The concession kept for them in punishment compared to free women was because they did not possess that protection which naturally belonged to family-based women. Muslims were given permission for marriage with others' slave-girls, and the objective of this too was to elevate the social standard of slave-girls; but since in this form there were apprehensions of conflict between ownership rights and marriage rights, caution was emphasized in this type of marriage. 

The Source of the Punishment of Stoning (Rajm): Regarding this verse, a question also arises: this verse generalizes the limit (Hadd) of adultery described in Surah An-Nur for all types of adulterers, whether married or unmarried; so what is the source of the punishment of stoning for adultery? We will discuss this question, God willing, in Surah Al-Ma'idah and Surah An-Nur.

Exegesis of Verses 26 to 28:
Subject of Verses 26 to 28: In the middle of the commands and instructions related to social reform, these three verses appeared as a warning and reminder. Their purpose is, on one hand, to draw the Muslims' attention to the great value and worth of these commands—that Allah Almighty is transferring the inheritance of all prophets and righteous ones to you, so value it with a sincere heart and become worthy of Divine mercy; and on the other hand, to inform of the storm of opposition rising against these reforms from that interest-seeking class which sat with a predatory grip over the rights of orphans, widows, the weak, and slaves, and was not ready to withdraw from this grip in any way. In this light, recite these verses.

'yuridullahu liyubayyina lakum wa yahdiyakum sunanal-ladhina min qablikum wa yatuba 'alaykum wallahu 'alimum hakim'.

Meaning of the word 'Aradah' (Will/Intent): Reflecting on the style of 'yuridullahu liyubayyina lakum...' reveals a prominent difference: in one place, 'L' (Li) follows 'Yurid', and in another place, 'An'. This difference is not without benefit. From an investigation of both styles in the Holy Quran, the reality is clarified that the word 'Aradah' is used in two meanings. One, in the meaning of a 'definite decision' and 'final intent'; second, in the meaning of 'desiring.' When the first meaning is intended, it is followed by 'L'; and when it comes in the meaning of mere desiring, it is followed by 'An'. For example: 'Allah only intends to remove [liyudh-hiba] from you the impurity, O people of the [Prophet's] household...' (Al-Ahzab: 33). 'Allah intends to purify [liyutah-hirakum] you and complete His favor upon you...' (Al-Ma'idah: 6). 'Allah only intends to punish [liyu'adh-dhibahum] them through them in the life of this world...' (At-Tawbah: 55).

After clarifying this style, reflect on the meaning of the verse under discussion. First, it was clarified that Allah, through His knowledge and wisdom, has chosen you for this purpose: to clarify His verses and His commands and instructions for you, and to grant you anew the guidance of those paths of faith and righteous action which were opened for the world through the prophets and righteous ones but had now been lost—so that you turn toward Allah and Allah casts a look of mercy upon you. This point was expressed as a Divine decision because raising an Ummah through the final advent—which would be the bearer of the entire religion and Sharia and the heir of all predecessors and successors—was already settled in Allah Almighty's scheme, and previous prophets had given news of it, and its being raised was also a requirement of God's knowledge and wisdom; for He, the All-Knowing and Wise, could not like that He leave His created beings to wander in misguidance without making any arrangement for their guidance.

The purpose of expressing this point as a final decision is, first, the encouragement of the Muslims; second, the discouragement of those enemies and opponents of Islam who, due to these social reforms mentioned in this Surah, had pursued the Muslims like the thorns of a bush. Social reforms are generally not tolerated with a cool heart by the interest-seeking class. For this reason, a fire was lit among the Jews, Christians, and polytheists by these reforms. Those who also saw that the strike of these reforms was falling upon their unbridled freedoms and unrestrained lust-worship rose with the intention to fail all these reforms and push God's creation back into that same pit of darkness from which Islam had shown this light to rescue them. Similarly, those who had loaded the burdens of their self-invented Sharias and home-made customs and traditions upon themselves—when they saw that these burdens were being removed from people's heads and the unnatural shackles of 'Isr and Aghlal' were being cut—they began screaming that the entire heritage of the ancestors was in danger. In response to all of them, the Quran told the Muslims: do not care for the nonsense of these opponents. The original inheritance of the previous prophets and righteous ancestors is this very one being transferred to you. God has wanted to bless you with mercy, but these wicked and corrupt ones want to deprive you of this mercy. In the final verse, the hint was also made that the shackles being cut by these reforms are being cut because they were unnatural and self-invented. The sound nature (fitrah salimah) upon which Providence created the human cannot bear these unnatural burdens. Further on in this Surah, from verse 43, the detail of all these oppositions is coming.

Section 14: Subject of Verses 29 to 33: After the incidental reminder and warning in the previous verses, the subject of commands and instructions related to social reform has resumed in these verses, and a few such instructions have been given which both support and validate the previous reforms and expand the scope of these reforms. In this light, recite the following verses.

'ya-ayyu-hal-ladhina amanu la ta'kulu amwalakum baynakum bil-batili illa an takuna tijaratan 'an taradhim-minkum wa la taqtulu anfusakum innallaha kana bikum rahima'.

There is an extremely deep relationship between the Sanctity of Property and the Sanctity of Life: In this verse, consuming each other's wealth through 'Batil' (false/unjust) methods and the killing of the soul (qatl-e-nafs) were made Haram, and both these prohibitions were gathered together. The wisdom of gathering them together is that there is a very deep relationship between the two. The greed for wealth removes the distinction between lawful and unlawful methods of obtaining it, and then this disease blinds people such that it leads to murder and bloodshed. If the causes of social disorders and blood-sheddings are traced, it will be known that the greed for wealth has the primary role in them. Due to this deep mutual relationship between these two things, Islam has emphasized the sanctity of each other's wealth and each other's life equally: 'hurmatu malihi ka-hurmati damihi' (the sanctity of a believer's wealth is like the sanctity of his blood).

True Mutual Consent of the Parties is a Condition for any Transaction: 'Batil' methods refer to those methods of transaction, business, and trade in which the true consent of both parties to the transaction is not found equally; rather, in them, the interest of one is secured while the other becomes a target of harm (dharar) or uncertainty (gharar). The Quran itself has hinted at this meaning. Consequently, after the prohibition of consuming each other's wealth through 'Batil' methods, the statement 'except that it be trade by mutual consent among you' is the clarification of the summary found in the first segment from the positive aspect. This segment clarified that only when the foundation of transaction and exchange is on true mutual consent is the profit obtained from it lawful; and if some deception is found in it or the helplessness and compulsion of one party has a role, then although they may apparently be consenting, this falls under the ruling of 'consuming wealth through Batil.' On this basis, all those forms of transaction and trade in which there is a taint of harm or uncertainty were declared unlawful in Islam. Riba (usury) is one of the most repulsive forms of this business. Cast a glance at the discussion we wrote in the exegesis of Al-Baqarah regarding the difference between Riba and trade.

Meaning of 'la taqtulu anfusakum': 'la taqtulu anfusakum' (do not kill yourselves/each other) is a segment of equal weight to 'la ta'kulu amwalakum baynakum bil-batil'—meaning neither devour each other's wealth nor kill each other. There is neither any occasion nor place here for taking its meaning as suicide, nor is there any room for that meaning in these words. If the meaning of suicide were to be expressed, the style would be entirely different. The word 'anfusakum' (yourselves) is for the expression of the reality that all members of society are one's own brothers; on the principle of 'the Muslim is the brother of the Muslim' and 'the Muslims are all brothers,' the killer of one is the killer of all. For this reason, the Quran has designated the killer of one as the killer of all.

'innallaha kana bikum rahima'. The Demands of God's attribute of Mercy: 'innallaha kana bikum rahima' (Indeed, Allah is to you Merciful) is in reality the cause stated for those prohibitions mentioned above. Meaning: when your Lord is Kind and Merciful to you, how can He like that you devour each other's wealth and kill each other? A Kind and Merciful Lord would only want that you live as 'merciful among themselves.' Then from here the point also emerged that if, contrary to this, people commit oppression and transgression among themselves, then it is the very demand of His kindness and mercy that He brings such a Day of Justice in which He brings to justice those who were guilty of it. Consequently, the next verse points toward this reality.

'wa may-yaf'al dhalika 'udwanaw-wa zhulman fa-sawfa nushlihi nara wa kana dhalika 'alallahi yasira'.

'Dhalika' (that) refers to both those things mentioned in the previous verse.

Specific Meaning of 'Udwan' and 'Zhulm': When the words 'Udwan' (transgression) and 'Zhulm' (oppression/wrongdoing) appear together, as we have written elsewhere, they denote two separate forms of sin. One form is that a person commits an encroachment upon another's life or property through force and violence; the second form is that a person, through high-handedness, does not fulfill someone's mandatory right but suppresses it. The first form is 'Udwan', the second is 'Zhulm'. If they appear separately, they encompass each other's meaning.

'Naran' (a Fire) is used as an indefinite noun for 'magnification' (tafkhim)—meaning We will cast such people into a severely blazing fire.

A Subtle Point: 'wa kana dhalika 'alallahi yasira' (and that is for Allah easy) points toward a hidden reality. It is that those who believe in God but do not hold the correct concept of His attributes of justice and mercy are very generous in giving themselves 'allowances.' They continue to commit great crimes yet, like the Jews, expect that God is very Kind to them and will therefore forgive everything. The statement of the Jews 'it will be forgiven for us' which the Quran quoted is indicative of this very mindset. In reality, people of this caliber obtain encouragement from the respite and delay which Allah Almighty grants such people according to His Sunnah, but their pretext-seeking nature seeks the cover of God's mercy. Even though God is Merciful, why after all would He have mercy on the oppressors? The original rightful claimants of His mercy are those oppressed who were persecuted by them throughout their lives and could not even sigh. Allah Almighty, addressing those holding such a mindset, stated that plunging those living a life of oppression and transgression into Hell will not be at all heavy upon the Merciful God. This is because just as He is Merciful, He is also Just, and this justice is also a demand of His mercy.

'in tajtanibu kaba'ira ma tunhawna 'anhu nukaffir 'ankum sayyi'atikum wa nudkhilkum-mudkhalan karima'.

Meaning of 'Kabair' (Major Sins) and 'Saghair' (Minor Sins): Since the word 'sayyi'at' (evil deeds) appears here opposite 'kaba'ir' (major sins), it refers to 'saghair'—meaning small sins. Just as the good deeds commanded are both large and small, similarly the evils prohibited are both small and large. Although this being small or large also keeps changing with the change of circumstances and relationships—making their logical boundary-setting somewhat difficult—nonetheless, this is not something difficult to understand. Just as migration (Hijrah) and Jihad are good deeds, and removing a painful thing from the path is also a good deed, but there is a great difference between the two and every person understands this difference; similarly, looting someone's house is an evil, and throwing something filthy in the path or spitting in a wrong place is also an evil, but there is a 'heaven and earth' difference between the two evils, and every person understands this difference too. In reality, the scale for measuring the greatness or smallness of both good deeds and evils is their effects and results; if our gaze is far-reaching and we reflect on facts while being superior to the partiality of the soul's desires, no confusion can arise in understanding this. But sometimes it happens that such a dominance of the soul's desires occurs over the reason that a mountain becomes a molehill and a molehill a mountain. To save from this confusion, the Sharia clarified both the Haram and the Halal. But between them are some things regarding which confusion can arise. Regarding such things, the demand of Taqwa is indeed that a person adopts the side of caution, but if a mistake is issued due to human weakness, Allah Almighty does not allow its dross to settle on the heart of one who respects the limits of Halal and Haram.

The Path to Paradise: In this verse, the reality was clarified that the method for escaping Divine seizure and entering His Paradise is not to give yourself 'allowances' with great open-heartedness, but the path is to abstain from the 'Kabair' (major sins) of the things He has prohibited. If you abstain from the major sins, He will personally remove the minor sins through His favor and mercy; otherwise, both major and minor sins will be recorded in your book of deeds and you will have to give an account of all of them.

The Path to escaping Minor Sins is also Abstaining from Major Sins: Here, this reality should also be remembered: the path to escaping minor sins is also that a person abstains from major sins. A person who pays off their debts of thousands is never pleased with the disgrace of being called a non-payer for suppressing someone's five rupees. In contrast, those who commit major sins but observe great care for small good deeds—their state throughout life remains that they are 'straining the gnat and swallowing the camel.' They are very meticulous in explaining the account of Zakat for cumin and fennel to others, but they themselves build their villas and decorate them from the wealth of orphans and the incomes of endowments (awqaf).

'wa la tatamannaw ma fadhalallahu bihi ba'dhakum 'ala ba'dh lir-rijali nashibum-mim-maktasabu wa lin-nisa'i nashibum-mim-maktasabna was'alullaha min fadhlihi innallaha kana bi-kulli shay'in 'alima'.

The Original Field of Competition is the Field of Acquired Attributes: Countless struggles in society arise because people are not aware of which field is for trying one's luck and struggle and which is not. The result is that wrong aspirations and a blind, deaf ambition throw people into such fields where a person's entire struggle and all their capability and potential are sacrificed to a futile clash and useless dispute. Allah Almighty has given some people preference over others in terms of their 'creative' (khalqi) attributes. For example: He created some beautiful, some ugly; some with sound limbs, some with defective limbs; some in wealthy households, some in poor households. Obviously, these things are creative. Competition and rivalry in them can give nothing but bitterness and unpleasantness. Similarly, He made someone a man, someone a woman; this thing too is creative. If a woman tries to become a man or a man a woman, this too is pure foolishness. By the same analogy, God has determined limits and rights for everyone in His law. These rights and limits are based on nature and wisdom. If in the wrong zeal of seeking equality, an attempt is made to jump over these rights and limits—the woman wanting to receive a share equal to the man, relatives wanting everyone's rank to be the same—this too is a war with nature and God's wisdom, the result of which can be nothing other than the entire system being turned upside down.

Today, the disorder and chaos, the clash and competition, and the murder and looting in the world are mostly the result of this wrong thinking and lack of boundary-recognition. The Quran stated in this verse that the field of competition is not that of creative attributes or natural preferences but the field of 'acquired' (iktisabi) attributes. This field is the field of goodness, Taqwa, worship, discipline, repentance, turning to God, or in comprehensive words, faith and righteous action.
