There is no barrier for anyone to advance in this: the man may advance, he will receive the full fruit of his struggle; the woman may advance, she will receive the fruit of her effort; free, slave, lady, handmaiden, noble, base, blind, seeing—this field is equally open for all. If there are some natural and creative obstacles in someone, the compensation for that lack is also present here. The favor (Fadhl) of God here is thousands and millions of times greater than the preferences He distributed creatively; so those seeking preference should enter this field and seek God's favor ('was'alullaha min fadhlihi'). The Giver is aware of everyone's quest, everyone's taste and passion, everyone's intention, and everyone's sincerity; and there is no lack in His treasury, nor is He miserly in giving; so what is gained by wasting one's labor in the wrong field? Whoever wants to try their luck should do so in this field. 'And for this let the competitors compete.'

'wa li-kullin ja'alna mawaliya mimma tarakal-walidani wal-aqrabun wal-ladhina 'aqadat aymanukum fa-atuhum nashibahum innallaha kana 'ala kulli shay'in shahida'.

Meaning of the word 'Mawla': The word 'Mawla' comes in very broad meanings in Arabic. Its determination is mostly by the occasion, place, and context. Here, the context proves that it refers to the heirs of every deceased person.

God's Appointed Heirs are the Original Heirs: This verse points toward that code of inheritance distribution mentioned in verse 7. 'For men is a share of what the parents and close relatives leave, and for women is a share of what the parents and close relatives leave...', the purpose of this reference is to further emphasize it: that the heirs God has appointed for every deceased person are the original heirs. Now, based on one's personal inclinations, there is neither room for any amendment or change in them, nor for any increase or decrease in their appointed shares. If someone has made a promise to give something to a non-heir, they should give them the portion that is theirs—'their portion' obviously refers to that portion for which the deceased is permitted to make a will and which Allah Almighty has kept separate from His own distribution. This portion is in reality left by Allah Almighty Himself for such people. For this reason, the word 'nasibahum' (their portion) was used for it. Finally, He referenced His attribute 'over all things a Witness' as a warning: even the most hidden attempt at improper partiality cannot remain hidden from Allah's knowledge. He is present and observant in every place and aware of every manifest and hidden.

Section 16: Subject of Verses 34, 35:
Instructions for the Education of the Family: In 'wa la tatamannaw...', the instruction given to both woman and man to struggle for attaining felicity and perfection while staying within their respective natural and Shari limits—that instruction is now designated as the guiding principle for the formation and organization of family life, and instructions for the organization of the family are being given. It should be remembered here that society is composed of families, and the state emerges from society. Effectively, the family is the thing which is the fundamental brick of society and then the state. For this reason, it is extremely necessary that this first brick be placed very correctly. If it becomes even slightly tilted, then (verse): 'the wall goes tilted up to the Pleiades.' In this light, recite the following verses.

'ar-rijalu qawwamuna 'alan-nisa'i bi-ma fadhalallahu ba'dhahum 'ala ba'dhiw-wa bi-ma anfaqu min amwalihim fas-salihâtu qanitâtun hafizhâtul-lil-ghaybi bi-ma hafizhallahu wal-lati takhafuna nushuzahunna fa-'izhu-hunna wahjuruhunna fil-madhadhi'i wadribuhunna fa-in ata'nakum fala tabghu 'alayhinna sabila innallaha kana 'aliyyan kabira'.

Meaning of 'qama 'ala': In Arabic, when 'qama' is followed by 'ala', the subject of monitoring, protection, maintenance, and custodianship is produced within it. In 'qawwamuna 'alan-nisa'', there is both the meaning of superiority and of maintenance and custodianship, and these two points are somewhat inseparable.

The Head of the State of the Home is the Man: The small unit of the home is also, as we hinted above, a small state. Just as every state is in need of a head for its survival and existence, similarly this state too is in need of a head. Now the question arose: should the position of leadership in this state be obtained by the man or the woman? The Quran answered that this position has been blessed to the man with preference over the woman. The man possesses a prominent superiority over the woman in certain attributes, on the basis of which he alone is worthy of the responsibility of 'Qawwam-ship' being placed upon him. For example, the power and capability for protection and defense, or the potential and courage for earning and 'striking with hands and feet' (laboring), which is within him, is not within the woman. It should be noted that the subject under discussion here is not 'total preference' but only that preference which proves the man's entitlement to 'Qawwam-ship.' There are some other aspects of the woman's preference too, but they do not relate to 'Qawwam-ship.' For example, the capability the woman possesses for managing the home and the nurturing and monitoring of children, the man does not possess. For this reason, the Quran said the point in a style of ambiguity here, from which both the man and woman being 'possessors of preference' from some aspect emerges. But from the aspect of 'Qawwam-ship', only the aspect of the man's preference is dominant.

Second, that the man has spent his wealth on the woman. Meaning: he has carried all the economic and maintenance responsibilities of the wife and children upon his head. Obviously, the man did not carry this responsibility accidentally or as a donation, but carried it because this responsibility is for him to carry. He alone possesses the capabilities for it and he alone can fulfill its right.

Good Wives are those who are Obedient and Secret-keepers for the Maintainer: After exalting the man to the position of 'Qawwam-ship', the attitude of good wives was stated: that they obey their maintainer with full submissiveness and protect his secrets and his honor and name. From this, the point automatically emerged that the women who, entirely contrary to this, are today applying force for the point that they will live not as women but as men in every field of life—they are not 'Salihât' (righteous) but 'Fasiqât' (wicked), and they have wanted to entirely turn upside down that system upon which all the blessings and prosperities of family life depend.

Meaning of 'hafizhâtul-lil-ghayb': I have taken the meaning of 'hafizhâtul-lil-ghayb' as: they are protectors of secrets. One reason for taking this meaning is that the word 'Ghayb' is famous for the meaning of 'secret.' Second reason is that the composition of the speech here is such that the meaning of 'behind the back' has no room. Third is that the issue of the trust of secrets between woman and man is the most important issue. These two are each other's natural trustees. Especially the woman's rank is such that she is such a 'knower of secrets' regarding the man's merits and flaws, his home, his wealth and property, and his honor and name, that if she were to come to tearing his veil, the man would be left entirely naked. For this reason, the Quran specifically mentioned this attribute. The addition of 'bi-ma hafizhallah' (by what Allah has guarded) alongside it aims to express the high lineage of this attribute: that upon this attribute of theirs is a reflection of God's attribute, because God too has protected the secrets of His servants and servant-girls, otherwise if He were to come to tearing people's veils, who is there who would remain capable of showing their face anywhere?

The Reality of 'Nushuz': 'Nushuz' means to raise the head, but the dominant usage of this word is for that defiance and rebellion which manifests from a woman against her husband. If it is manifest from a woman's attitude that she has set out on the path of rebellion, then because the man is the 'Qawwam', he has been given some disciplinary authorities for the woman's discipline. But it should be noted that the Quran has given these authorities only for the form when 'Nushuz' is feared. 'Nushuz', as we hinted, is not what every shortcoming or heedlessness or indifference or natural desire for expressing one's personality and one's opinion and taste is called. 'Nushuz' is for the woman to be seen taking some such step that challenges the man's 'Qawwam-ship' and from which there is an apprehension of disorder and lack of peace being produced in the kingdom of the home. If such a form is seen arising, the man can adopt three forms, and the Quran's style of expression is proof that a sequence and graduality are respected in these three.

The Man's Disciplinary Authorities in the case of 'Nushuz': The first stage is to give advice and reprimand. The word 'wa'zh' (admonition) is in the Quran, within which is also found the meaning of rebuke and reprimand to an extent. If it is seen that the matter does not work through this, the second stage is to abandon the informal type of mixing with them so they estimate that if they do not change their way, its results can be far-reaching. If the matter is seen not to be settled even by this, then in the final degree, the man also has the authority to give physical punishment. But this should only be to the extent that a teacher and disciplinarian can give to some student under their training. The Holy Prophet (PBUH) clarified its limit with the words 'ghayra mubarrih' (not severe/not leaving a mark), the meaning of which is that the punishment should not be such that it leaves a lasting effect.

Past Grudges should be Forgotten after Reform: This is the final limit of the man's disciplinary authorities. If its result turns out to be useful, the woman returning to the path of obedience instead of rebellion, then past grudges should be forgotten. Pretexts for taking revenge from her should not be sought. The man, in the presumption of his 'Qawwam-ship', should not forget this point: that the highest and greatest is God. When He, being the Maintainer of the heavens and the earth, overlooks the 'Nushuz' of all of us and forgives everyone's disobediences after repentance and reform, then why should servants take the tone of their 'Qawwam-ship' beyond the limit?

'wa in khiftum shiqaqa baynihima fab'athu hakamam-min ahlihi wa hakamam-min ahliha in yurida islahay-yuwaffiqillahu baynahuma innallaha kana 'aliman khabira'.

Another Strategy for Reforming the Situation: If the man, after doing all those efforts mentioned in the previous verse, could not gain control over the woman's 'Nushuz', then its clear meaning is that the gulf of difference is very vast and relations have reached the limit of breaking. But even after reaching this limit, the Sharia has not given the man permission to divorce the wife and get rid of her. Islam designates the bond of husband and wife as the foundation of society's stability. For this reason, it tolerates its breaking only when it is proven after adopting all possible strategies of reform that its remaining joined is now impossible or a cause of further corruption. Consequently, after the failure of the husband's efforts, the instruction was given to adopt a second strategy for reforming the situation. This instruction is given to the kith and kin, the community, and the relatives and well-wishers of the husband and wife: that they step forward and use their influences to try to reform this disorder. Its practical form told was: one arbiter (hakam) be chosen from the man's relatives, and a second from the wife's family; these two together should try for reform. Sometimes the dispute which the parties do not succeed in settling themselves is settled through the intervention of other well-wishers. The parties also have to respect their impartiality and well-wishing, and there is also the apprehension of others' reproach for being stubborn. For this reason, this form is more effective and result-oriented.

Urging the Husband and Wife toward Reconciliation: In 'in yurida islahay-yuwaffiqillahu baynahuma', although the arbiters can be intended, my inclination is toward the fact that the husband and wife themselves are intended—meaning if these two abandon their stubbornness and become seekers of reform, Allah Almighty will produce compatibility between them. This is in reality an extremely eloquent style of encouraging and urging the husband and wife to benefit from this opportunity and become seekers of compatibility from the Kind and Designing God instead of separation.

In this verse, in our view, the address—as we hinted—is to the kith and kin of the husband and wife and the elders of their nation and tribe, and the authority of these arbiters is limited entirely to the effort of reforming the situation. After the failure of this effort, the husband can personally take some step according to his Shari authorities and, if the matter is of a judicial nature, can also go to court. However, there is room that after a matter goes to court, it is referred by the court to some council (panchayat), and the court also delegates the authority of decision to the council.

Finally, the purpose of referencing the attributes of 'Alim' and 'Khabir' is to warn everyone: God is well-aware of what everyone's role has been in this dispute, and He will deal with them accordingly.

Subject of Verses 36 to 43: These are the verses of the conclusion of the chapter. The series of social commands and instructions that had been continuing from the beginning ends with these verses. Just as He began this chapter with the instruction to remain fearful of Allah, He ended it with the instruction to continue worshipping Allah. Allah Almighty's right is the greatest. Those who continue to fulfill this right as it should be fulfilled are in reality the ones who will be blessed with the success to fulfill others' rights. Consequently, after the reminder of this right, He gave a summary reminder of the rights of parents, relatives, orphans, the poor, neighbors, travelers, and slaves. Allah's right is His worship, and the thing that nullifies it is Shirk; for this reason, He negated Shirk along with the reminder of this right. The greatest right of servants is 'Ihsan' (excellence) toward them and 'Infaq' (spending) for them. Miserliness, arrogance, and hypocrisy are its destroyers; for this reason, He negated these things along with the emphasis on 'Ihsan' and 'Infaq'. After that, to encourage spending, He stated that this bargain is not a bargain of loss. Whoever spends one will receive ten. Then He issued a warning: the right of warning and preaching has been fulfilled through this Messenger; those who still do not listen should think that such a Day is also coming when Allah will gather all Messengers as witnesses over their nations and ask: what call did you give your respective nations and what response did they give? then this same question will be regarding this final Ummah from this final Messenger. That Day will be such that there will be no place of refuge for anyone, nor will anyone be able to hide any point.

Finally, He mentioned some etiquettes and conditions of prayer (Salah)—the greatest manifestation of the worship of Allah mentioned in the previous verse. The purpose of mentioning these etiquettes and conditions is to purify prayer from corruptors just as spending was purified from its obstacles and corruptors above. Now, in this light, recite verses 36 to 43.

'wa'budullaha wa la tushriku bihi shay'aw-wa bil-walidayni ihsanaw-wa bi-dhil-qurba wal-yatama wal-masakini wal-jari dhil-qurba wal-jaril-junubi wash-sahibi bil-janbi wabnis-sabili wa ma malakat aymanukum innallaha la yuhibbu man kana mukhtalan fakhura'.

In 'bil-walidayni ihsana', the 'B' is proof that the word 'Ihsan' here consists of the meaning of 'Birr'. Meaning: this excellence should be with the fulfillment of rights. It should not be merely an attempt to 'get it over with.' The use of 'B' is suitable only with the word 'Birr'. Thus, in Surah Maryam: 'wa barram-bi-walidayhi wa lam yakun jabbaran 'ashiyya' (And dutiful to his parents, and he was not a disobedient tyrant). In Urdu translations, the right of translating such styles is hardly fulfilled.

Three Types of Neighbors: 'al-jari dhil-qurba', meaning a neighbor who also has the connection of kinship. 'al-jaril-junub', 'Junub' means stranger—meaning a neighbor with whom the connection of kinship and proximity does not exist. 'ash-sahibi bil-janb', 'Janb' means side—whoever becomes your companion and fellow-traveler even temporarily in some assembly, some circle, some vehicle, some shop, or some hotel, is the 'Companion by the side.' In Islamic society, all three types of people obtain rights of neighborhood over each other.

God's Right is the Greatest: In these verses, first of all, Allah Almighty commanded the fulfillment of His right because, due to being the Creator, Master, and Lord, His right is the greatest, and the fulfillment of other rights depends on the fulfillment of this right. Those who do not fulfill God's right are also not blessed with the success to fulfill others' rights correctly. God's right is worship, and we clarified elsewhere that worship includes both adoration and obedience. For this worship, the condition is that no one be associated in it because there is no other partner in God's divinity. If some other is associated in this right, this worship remains void.

After God, the Greatest Right is of Parents: After God, the greatest right is of parents because Allah makes them the medium for a person's coming into existence and their nurturing. But their right is not worship but 'Birr' and 'Ihsan'. After that are the rights of relatives, which in reality emerge from this very right.

Rights of Relatives, the Poor, and Neighbors: Then are the rights of orphans, the poor, and neighbors. Neighbors can be of three types. One who is both a neighbor and a relative; second, one who is a neighbor although not a relative; third, one who has become a companion and fellow-traveler temporarily in some journey or residence. Excellence was instructed toward all of them. Then the traveler and slaves and slave-girls are mentioned. Regarding slave-girls and slaves, we have mentioned that slavery is not a part of Islam's system; Islam tolerated it under the international circumstances of the time and personally prescribed such forms of slaves' welfare and progress in its system that they would gradually become equal members of the Islamic society. In this verse, they too were included among the recipients of excellence, and the objective of this is that people's angle of vision regarding them changes and people keep their reform and progress as an independent issue in opportunities of virtue and excellence.

Mindset contrary to the Fulfillment of Rights: 'innallaha la yuhibbu man kana mukhtalan fakhura'. This is the description of the mindset contrary to the fulfillment of rights and excellence. The meaning is: those who consider the abundance of means and resources as Allah's favor and blessing—within them the motive of gratitude and humility arises, and this motive urges them to do excellence toward others just as Allah Almighty has done excellence toward them; consequently, they do excellence toward people and become worthy of Allah Almighty's love. In contrast, those who begin to consider the blessings given by Allah Almighty as a miracle of their own power, capability, strategy, and wisdom—within them, instead of the motive of humility and gratitude, pride and boasting are produced, and instead of doing excellence toward people, they try to impose their dominance and awe over them. Allah Almighty does not love such ungrateful and small-hearted ones. "Does not love" means He hates such people.

'al-ladhina yabkhaluna wa yamurunan-nasa bil-bukhli wa yaktumuna ma atahumullahu min fadhlihi wa a'tadna lil-kafirina 'adhabam-muhina'.

These are a few more characteristics of those who exult and boast, which we will clarify in order:

Further Characteristics of those who Exult and Boast: First, they are themselves miserly and also advise others toward miserliness. A 'miser' (bakhil) is that person who is narrow-hearted in fulfilling others' rights. A person who fulfills others' rights with generosity and open-heartedness but practices caution and tightness regarding their own self is not called a miser. The major cause of miserliness is that a person begins to consider their wealth and resources as a miracle of their own strategy and capability instead of God's gift. For this reason, that very motive of humility and gratitude which is the original driver of generosity and bounty dies within them. A miserly person also advises others toward miserliness; the reason for this is that others' generosity exposes the secret of their own miserliness. To keep a veil over this defect of theirs, their effort is that just as they sit suppressing others' rights, others also remain sitting—so that no one will be 'with a nose' (honorable), and they won't have to become the 'nose-less' one (disgraced). It is the rule that the person who is a coward also gives lessons of cowardice to others so that the secret of their own cowardice is not outed.

A Psychological Aspect of Miserly Wealthy People: Second, they hide that favor which Allah has given them. This points toward an extremely hidden psychological aspect of miserly wealthy people. The desire of miserly wealthy people is, on one hand, that their dominance of sovereignty and wealth remains established over everyone; on the other hand, they also keep making the effort that no person can give them any reproach in the matter of fulfilling rights. Consequently, they keep crying out their 'tale of woe'—their vast expenses, losses in business, their spread-out responsibilities, and the abundance of seekers and supplicants—before every acquaintance and every seeker of resources, so that people think: "this person is generous and high-spirited, but the poor man—what can he do? He is buried under the weight of very heavy responsibilities," and thus despite possessing an income of thousands and millions, nothing remains with him.

'wa a'tadna lil-kafirina 'adhabam-muhina': Regarding such ungrateful deniers of favor, He stated: We have prepared a humiliating punishment. A humiliating punishment because they, after receiving Allah's blessing, became exulters, boasters, and hiders of His favor instead of being His grateful and right-fulfilling servants.

'wal-ladhina yunfiquna amwalahum ri'a'an-nasi wa la yu'minuna billahi wa la bil-yawmil-akhir wa may-yakunish-shaytanu lahu qarinan fa-sa'a qarina'.

Showy Spending is a Business Expense: This too is a point of the same series mentioned above. Meaning: if they spend, they do so merely for show. Showy spending is a business expense. First, its benefit rarely reaches those who are the original rightful claimants, because in the matter of the original rightful claimants, there isn't much opportunity for display and show. Then, like the hypocrite's prayer, the spending of display also has no connection with Allah. These types of people are claimants of faith in God and the Hereafter, but in reality, neither is their faith on God nor on the Hereafter, and spending is authoritative with Allah Almighty only when it is accompanied by faith in God and the Hereafter, because that spending alone is a cause of goodness and blessing for this world and that spending alone will be a cause of goodness and blessing in the Hereafter. Those who are empty of faith in God and the Hereafter—their companion becomes Satan, and for one whose companion becomes Satan, no possibility remains for doing any work of goodness and blessing. 'Indeed, the wasteful are brothers of the devils, and ever is Satan to his Lord ungrateful' (Al-Isra: 27).

Exegesis of Verses 39-40: 'wa madha 'alayhim law amanu billahi wal-yawmil-akhiri wa anfaqu mimma razaqahumullah wa kanallahu bihim 'alima. innallaha la yazhlimu mithqala dharrah wa in taku hasanatay-yudha'ifha wa yu'ti mil-ladunhu ajran 'azhima'.

This is an expression of sorrow at the misfortune of these narrow-hearted and miserly ones: that they consider it a great loss to believe in Allah and the Hereafter and to spend that very wealth He blessed them with in Allah's path. Even though this is not a bargain of loss but entirely profit upon profit. Allah Almighty is neither unaware of anyone's action, nor is He one to commit even an atom's weight of injustice toward anyone; rather, if someone has any good deed, He will multiply it many times and upon that, He will personally bestow a very great reward from Himself.

Exegesis of Verses 41 to 42: 'fa-kayfa idha ji'na min kulli ummatim-bi-shahidiw-wa ji'na bika 'ala ha'ula'i shahida. yawma'idhiy-yawad-dul-ladhina kafaru wa 'ashawur-rasula law tusawwa bihimul-ardhu wa la yaktumunallaha haditha'.

The Prophets' Testimony over their Nations in the Resurrection: The meaning is: as far as the finality of proof (itmam al-hujjah) is concerned, Allah has informed them of His religion through His Messenger and completed the proof over them. Now, in the matter of informing of the religion and Sharia, no lack remains. After this finality of proof, if these people still remain deviated from Allah and the Messenger, then today they can hide their Kufr and hypocrisy, but what will they do tomorrow when Allah gathers all nations and their prophets in the field of Resurrection and has the prophets give testimony that they had delivered the religion to the people, and you (the address is to the Prophet PBUH) will be stood to give similar testimony over the people of this Ummah. On that Day, all those who committed Kufr and disobedience to the Messenger will wish that they were swallowed by the earth and the earth were made level with them. On that Day, no person will be able to hide any point from God.

'la yaktumunallaha haditha' (they will not hide from Allah any statement): the negation of the verb is in the meaning of the inability of the verb. They will speak out because of the inability to hide anything. The Quran clarified this reality in another place thus: 'Until, when they reach it, their hearing and their eyes and their skins will testify against them...' (Fussilat: 20). Here, within this word, a subtle critique is also hidden. These people hide that favor of Allah which Allah has given them—'wa yaktumuna ma atahumullahu min fadhlihi'. Here it stated: on that Day, they will not be able to hide anything from Allah. Everything will be unveiled and become speaking to give testimony. 

The point that prophets will be stood as witnesses over their respective nations on the Day of Resurrection is also proven from other places in the Holy Quran. In Surah Al-Ma'idah: '[Be warned of] the Day when Allah will assemble the messengers and say, "What was the response you received?" They will say, "We have no knowledge. Indeed, it is You who is Knower of the unseen."' (Al-Ma'idah: 109). Meaning: Allah Almighty will ask the messengers on the Day of Resurrection: when you delivered Allah's religion to your respective nations, how did they deal with the religion? The messengers will respond: we delivered Your religion to the people without any addition or subtraction; how they dealt with this religion—its knowledge is with You alone because the Knower of the unseen is You. 

The full reality of this testimony is clarified by Sayyidina Jesus's testimony, which is mentioned in Surah Al-Ma'idah thus: 'And [beware the Day] when Allah will say, "O Jesus, Son of Mary, did you say to the people, 'Take me and my mother as deities besides Allah?'" He will say, "Exalted are You! It was not for me to say that to which I have no right. If I had said it, You would have known it. You know what is within myself, and I do not know what is within Yourself. Indeed, it is You who is Knower of the unseen. I said not to them except what You commanded me - to worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord. And I was a witness over them as long as I was among them; but when You took me up, You were the Observer over them, and You are, over all things, Witness."' (Al-Ma'idah: 116-117).

Regarding this verse of An-Nisa, it comes in traditions that once when Ibn Mas'ud (RA) reached this verse while reciting the Quran to the Prophet (PBUH), the Prophet's eyes overflowed with tears due to the intensity of the impression. This clarifies that this verse carries an extremely great responsibility alongside an extremely great honor for the Prophet (PBUH).

'ya-ayyu-hal-ladhina amanu la taqrabu-s-salata wa antum sukara hatta ta'lamu ma taquluna wa la junuban illa 'abiri sabilin hatta taghtashilu wa in kuntum-mardha aw 'ala safarin aw ja'a ahadum-minkum-minal-gha'iti aw lamastumun-nisa'a falam tajidu ma'an fatayammamû sha'idan tayyiban famshahu bi-wujuhikum wa aydikum innallaha kana 'afuwwan ghafura'.

'Salah' means prayer, but just as sometimes the container (zharf) is spoken and the contained (mazhruf) is automatically included in its meaning, similarly sometimes the contained—if clues exist—encompasses the container too. Here two clues exist which are proof that the word 'Salah' also encompasses the 'place of Salah', i.e., the mosque. First, He stated: in the state of intoxication and Janaba (major ritual impurity), do not even go near the Salah. Obviously, if 'Salah' meant merely the prayer, it was sufficient to say "do not pray." The special benefit of expressing this objective with the words 'la taqrabu' (do not approach) was not understood. Second, it is accompanied by the exception 'illa 'abiri sabilin' (except those passing through). Meaning: if the mere passing through the place of prayer is intended, there is no harm in it. This 'passing through' does not have suitability with the prayer itself but its clear suitability can only be with the place of prayer. Some, to avoid this lack of suitability, took 'abiri sabilin' to mean the state of travel, but this is mere affectation. First, this expression is entirely alien for travel; second, the concession for the state of travel is described immediately after in this verse with the words 'aw 'ala safarin'. Why then would its mention be here?

'Sukara' is the plural of 'Sakran', referring to one drunk with the intoxication of wine. By stopping from prayer and the place of prayer in the state of intoxication, Islam took this initial step on the path of prohibiting wine. Among the Jews, the prohibition of wine was only for the priests during times of worship. From this, it was known that Islam's first step on this path is further ahead than their final step. 

By mentioning both intoxication and Janaba together and declaring both equally as 'corruptors of prayer,' the Quran has guided toward the reality that both these states are of impurity (najâsat); the only difference is that intoxication is the impurity of the reason and Janaba is that of the body. The Quran's calling wine 'rijs' (impurity) was thus clarified. 

Just as the word 'Junub' comes for 'stranger'—as passed above—it also comes for 'one in a state of major ritual impurity,' and its form remains the same in singular, plural, masculine, and feminine. 

'Tayammum' means to intend and turn toward. 'Sa'id' refers to the surface of the earth. In the case of illness, travel, and water not being available, the instruction was given to find some pure and clean place and wipe the face and hands, to obtain purification; although this wiping holds no importance from the viewpoint of obtaining cleanliness, it holds great importance from the aspect of maintaining the memory of the original method of purification in the mind. In most acts of worship, the Sharia has respected this matter: when their fulfillment in the original form is impossible or difficult, their memorial is kept in a 'resembling' form so that when circumstances become correct, the readiness to return to them remains in the nature. 

Three Occasions for Tayammum: Three occasions for Tayammum are described here: illness, travel, and the unavailability of water. From this, the point automatically emerges that in the state of illness and travel, a person can perform Tayammum even while water is present. In illness, there is an apprehension of harm from Wudu or Ghusl; therefore, this concession occurred. Similarly, in travel, various situations can arise where a person has to suffice with Tayammum. For example: water is not unavailable but is scarce. There is a fear that if used for Ghusl, etc., water for drinking will run out; or there is a fear that if engaged in the arrangement of bathing, they will be separated from the companions of the caravan. Or it is a journey by rail or ship where bathing is a cause of severe trouble.

Permission for Tayammum in both states of Hadath (minor impurity) and Janaba (major impurity): Two states of impurity are mentioned here. One: 'aw ja'a ahadum-minkum-minal-gha'it' (or one of you comes from the place of relieving oneself). The word 'Gha'it' originally refers to low-lying land. here it is a metaphor for relieving oneself. This is because in simple rural life, people generally go to low-lying lands and bushes to relieve themselves. Second: 'aw lamastumun-nisa'a' (or you have touched women). 'Malamasat' originally means touching and placing the hand, but here it is a metaphor for sexual intercourse. The purpose of mentioning both these states of impurity is that Tayammum suffices for every type of impurity. If this clarification were not there, the doubt could arise that Tayammum is permissible in ordinary Hadath but not in the other form.

Context and Structure of the Verse: After clarifying the components, understand the occasion and structure of the verse once more. In verse 36, He commanded the worship of Allah alone and excellence and spending toward parents and relatives, etc. Alongside that, He mentioned the things that nullify worship and excellence/spending, such as Shirk and hypocrisy. Now, He mentioned those 'corruptors of prayer' (mufsidat-e-salat) which render the greatest manifestation of Divine worship—prayer—void. Above, Shirk was mentioned, which is creedal impurity. In this verse, He guided toward outward impurities and the strategies for their removal. He stated: do not go to prayer or the place of prayer in the state of intoxication and Janaba. In the state of intoxication—when a person has no awareness of what words they are uttering from the tongue and what covenant they are making with Allah regarding doing and not doing—performing prayer is a futile act. We hinted above that this verse was revealed in that era when the command for the absolute prohibition of wine had not been revealed. This command effectively warned people to train themselves for the absolute prohibition of wine. Similarly, He prohibited going to prayer or the place of prayer in the state of Janaba. This is a hint toward the fact that just as the intoxication of wine is a nullifier of prayer, the lethargy and constriction of Janaba are also contrary to the expansion and presence of heart required for prayer. Along with this prohibition, He kept the exception that if someone needs to merely pass through the place of prayer in this state, there is a concession for it. The purification for Janaba is Ghusl (bathing). But if someone is in illness or travel or is not finding water, they can perform Tayammum. He mentioned relieving oneself and sexual intercourse alongside this so that it becomes manifest that Tayammum is permissible in every type of impurity. The method of Tayammum told is to find some pure place and wipe the face and hands. Finally, He stated that Allah is Pardoning and Forgiving. Meaning: He granted this concession to servants because He is 'Afuww' and 'Ghafur'.

Subject of Verses 44 to 57: At verse 43, as we hinted above, the chapter of commands related to social reform ended. Further on is the statement of the reaction that manifested from the opponents of these reforms, and simultaneously the glad tiding of a great kingdom is being announced to the Muslims, which is the natural result of the society's maturity and perfection. Among the opponents, the Jews were taken first because, being People of the Book, they should have been the greatest supporters of these reforms, but unfortunately, the greatest opposition came from them. Consequently, after mentioning their hostile mischiefs, they were addressed directly and threatened: O People of the Book! If you do not believe in this Book, then remember that the time has come for you to be cursed like the 'People of the Sabbath' and for your faces to be wiped out.

Following this, the Jews were rebuked for some of their polytheistic deeds and creeds and for their presumption that they are a chosen Ummah. Their thought is that whatever their creeds and deeds may be, they are the children of God's beloveds and will therefore take their place in Paradise without any accounting. It stated: this false presumption of theirs—which is entirely a fabrication against Allah—has made them completely indifferent to the responsibilities of faith and action, and they have removed themselves from the circle of servitude to include themselves in the category of divinity.

Following this, wonder was expressed at the fact that on one hand is this claim of sanctity and superiority, and on the other hand is such a state of mental and moral baseness that, being People of the Book, they believe in 'Jibt' and 'Taghut' and have become so blinded by envy against the Muslims that they declare even the disbelievers and polytheists as more rightly guided than the Muslims. It stated: if they are becoming blind with envy, let them be; now the decision of Divine destiny has been made that Allah will surely make the progeny of Ishmael the heirs of the Book, Wisdom, and a great Caliphate.

Following this, those among the progeny of Ishmael who accepted this call were encouraged, and those who persisted in its opposition were threatened with the punishment of the Hereafter. In this light, recite these verses.

'alam tara ilal-ladhina utu nashibam-minal-kitabi yashtarunadh-dhalalata wa yuriduna an tadhillus-sabil'. The Relationship between the Quran and other Heavenly Scriptures is that of Part and Whole: The address 'Alam tara', as we have written elsewhere, generally comes for a collective and for expressing wonder and sorrow. Here the address is to the Muslims, and 'those who were given a portion of the Scripture' refers to the Jews. We clarified elsewhere that the relationship between previous heavenly scriptures and the Great Quran is that of a part to a whole. The Holy Quran is Allah Almighty's perfect Book; other heavenly scriptures hold the status of its components and portions. For this reason, the greatest expectation from those who were made bearers of the components and portions of this perfect Book was that when this perfect Book came to them, they would step forward to welcome it; but their state is strange: they prefer misguidance over this guidance, and let alone accepting it, their heart-and-soul effort is that you too lose the straight path you have found. The hint passed in verse 27 above that Allah Almighty is guiding toward the ways of previous prophets and righteous ones through this Book, but the followers of the soul's desires are trying to make you move completely away from the path of truth. Now the details of that hint are coming.

'wallahu a'lamu bi-a'da'ikum wa kafa billahi waliyyaw-wa kafa billahi nashira'. This is a sentence of tranquility and consolation for the Muslims. The meaning is: Allah Almighty is not unaware of these enemies of yours. He is well-aware of them and their tricks and mischiefs; He will fail their every mischief. For one whose Ally and Supporter is Allah, Allah's support and victory are sufficient. So continue advancing on your path and rely upon Allah's design and help.

'minal-ladhina hadu yuharrifunal-kalima 'am-mawadhi'ihi wa yaquluna sami'na wa 'asayna wasma' ghayra musma'iw-wa ra'ina layyam-bi-alsinatihim wa ta'nan fid-din wa law annahum qalu sami'na wa ata'na wasma' wanzhurna lakana khayral-lahum wa aqwama wa lakil-la'anahumullahu bi-kufrihim fala yu'minuna illa qalila'.

All the words of this verse have been discussed in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah. This is a summary hint toward those mischiefs which the Jewish wicked ones used to commit to lower the Holy Prophet (PBUH) in people's eyes and to make Islam weightless and humble.

A Mischief of the Jews: Using good social words as sarcasm: The words 'sami'na wa ata'na', 'isma'', and 'ra'ina', etc., as we clarified in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah, were among the social words of the Arabs which served as proof of the listener's appreciation and valuing of the speaker, their expression of zeal and passion, and their acknowledgment and acceptance. Just as we say: "well said," "I submit," "listen, what a fine point has been said," "rare nuance," "please repeat," "say it again." Similarly, the aforementioned words and phrases were prevalent in Arabia. These words are originally for expressing praise or acknowledgment and acceptance, but if a group wants to commit mischief and ill-manners, they can very easily turn praise into disparagement and acknowledgment into mockery and sarcasm by merely twisting the tongue, distorting the pronunciation, or creating a slightly artificial tone in the voice. Whether this harms the speaker's dignity or not, the mischief-seeking individuals feel happy by trying to vent their frustration in this way. Now understand these words with some clarification.

Meaning of 'sami'na wa ata'na': The literal meaning is: we heard and we obeyed. The Arabs said this on the occasion when they wanted to express their readiness and preparedness for the fulfillment of an order upon the command and instruction of some elder, some chief, or some king. In Arabic, there is also the word 'ta'ah' for this, which has been used in the Quran too. The Jewish wicked ones would go to the Holy Prophet's (PBUH) assemblies and, to display their good-fortune and loyalty, would say 'sami'na wa ata'na' at every point; but through the disposal of the tone of voice, they would utter it such that they turned 'ata'na' (we obeyed) into 'asayna' (we disobeyed). Since the letters of both are harmonious and close in articulation (makhraj), they achieved success in this alteration. In this way, they would mold a sentence of submission and obedience into the mold of disobedience and rebellion, and the listeners could not even seize upon their mischief because they could very easily make the excuse that they said 'sami'na wa ata'na'. Obviously, in such a situation, a noble and self-respecting person considers it better to ignore the point after hearing and understanding it.

Meaning of 'isma' ghayra musma'': The literal meaning is: listen to a point that was not heard before. The good place of usage for this phrase is for a listener to draw another listener's attention after hearing some wise point of a speaker or orator: "listen to this wise and sagacious point; this point has been heard by our ears for the first time; never before has this point been heard." Obviously, this is not only proof of the speaker's and orator's appreciation but also an encouragement and urging for others toward their appreciation. But if someone says this very point in a tone of 'hooting' and in a manner of mockery, its meaning could also be: "listen to his 'unheard-of' [nonsense]; what a 'bird-in-the-air' he is flying; when would anyone have ever heard such a point!" Obviously, merely the change of manner and tone turned this extremely high phrase into a poison-tipped lancet of taunt and sarcasm; but no seizure can occur on this either, because if seized, the speaker can offer the defense that they said it for praise, not for sarcasm. Since the aspect of sarcasm in this phrase was produced from the words 'ghayra musma'', the Quran broke this tip and instructed that only 'isma'' (listen) be said.

Meaning of 'ra'ina': The literal meaning is: please have some consideration for us. The good place of usage for this word is when the addressee has not heard or understood the speaker's point well, or the point is so subtle and wise that they want to hear it again from the speaker's own tongue; then to draw attention again, just as we say "please say it again," similarly in Arabic they say 'ra'ina'. This word is proof of the listener's zeal and passion and their desire for knowledge. But the Jewish wicked ones, through 'layy al-lisan' (twisting of the tongue), would mold this too into sarcasm. The form of this would be: if you slightly suppress the 'kasrah' (vowel) of the 'Ain' in 'ra'ina', this word becomes 'ra'ina', and its meaning will be "our shepherd." Due to this mischief of the Jews, the Quran removed this word entirely from the social words of the Muslims and instructed the use of 'unzhurna' in its place, which means "please grant us respite" or "please pay attention again." Meaning: in terms of objective, this is exactly the substitute for 'ra'ina', and there is no opportunity in it for any corruption to be produced through the distortion of the tone. (Cast a glance at what we wrote in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah under verse 104; we have also shed light on the benefits of this social reform there).

Finally, it stated: this audacity and ill-manners which these people are committing against the final Prophet while being a bearer-of-the-Book group—this is not just like that; rather, this is the result of Allah's curse which occurred upon them due to their Kufr. God has driven them away from His door. Now, only with difficulty will the wealth of faith be blessed to some of them. 

Taunt against the Prophet is Taunt against the Religion itself: There is another point worthy of attention in this verse. It is that all these mischiefs of the Jews were of the nature of sarcasm against the Prophet (PBUH), but the Quran described them with the words 'ta'nan fid-din' (a taunt against the religion), which aims to point toward the reality that the Prophet is in reality the embodiment of the religion and the manifestation of the Sharia; therefore, a taunt against him is a taunt against the religion itself. We will discuss this nuance, God willing, in the exegesis of Surah Al-Hadid.

'ya-ayyu-hal-ladhina utul-kitaba aminu bi-ma nazzalna musaddiqal-lima ma'akum mim-ba'di an natmisa wujuhan fa-naruddaha 'ala adbariha aw nal'anahum kama la'anna as-habas-sabti wa kana amrullahi maf'ula'.

The Cause for Wiping Out Faces: 'tams ash-shay'' means to erase the traces and signs of a thing. Wiping out the faces means that these signs of the eye, ear, nose, and mouth be erased and made level, because Allah had blessed these powers for an extremely high purpose; but when that work was not taken from them for which they were granted—rather, entirely contrary to it, all these things have remained as pits for stumbling—then after all, why should these pits be kept remaining; why shouldn't they be filled? Note that in Surah Al-Baqarah, these people were called 'deaf, dumb, blind.' The meaning is: when despite possessing everything, they have become dumb, deaf, and blind, then they are deserving that these signs too be erased. 

Eloquence of the Indefiniteness of 'wujuhan': There is great eloquence in the indefiniteness (tankir) of 'wujuhan' (faces). This indefiniteness is for expressing hatred and loathing. In the previous verse, the mention of the curse upon them passed. Through this indefiniteness, the point is intended to be expressed that these known faces are to such a degree worthy of hatred that the speaker does not even like to point toward them with specificity. Consequently, He did not say 'wujuhahum' (their faces) but turned away from them and said 'wujuhan' (faces). This type of indefiniteness is also in the word 'qulub' (hearts) in 'a-fa-la yatadabbarunal-qur'ana am 'ala qulubin aqfaluha' (Muhammad: 24). We will discuss its eloquence in its place, God willing.

Meaning of 'fa-naruddaha 'ala adbariha': This is the detail of the point above. The meaning is: when there is no difference at all between their face and the back of their head—just as the rear part is flat, the front part too is practically flat—then why shouldn't this front part also be turned toward the rear.

The cause of the curse upon the 'People of the Sabbath' and its effects was discussed in detail under verses 65-66 of Al-Baqarah. 

Final Threat to the Jews: This verse is not a verse of invitation for the Jews but of threat and warning. The mention of invitation in it occurred merely for the finality of proof (itmam al-hujjah). The meaning is: now this is the final opportunity if you want to recover. This opportunity, once gone, will never be available again. It is better that you believe in this Book which has been revealed confirming the prophecies of your own Book. Otherwise, remember that for you that time has arrived when your faces be distorted or such a curse be made upon you as was made upon the people of the Sabbath, such that they remained as disgraced apes. 

The Curse of which the Jews were Deserving: Like mercy, curse and punishment also have various degrees. The degree of curse they are threatened with here is that curse of which they had in reality become deserving due to their mischiefs. If Allah Almighty made a curse of a lower degree upon them, this was effectively a little respite given to them; and every respite a nation receives—if they do not value it—becomes a cause for the increase in their afterlife punishment. 

Similarity between Deed and Punishment: While reciting this verse, the thought repeatedly occurs to me that in the threat of distorting the faces given to them, a similarity between deed and punishment is found. In the previous verse, that action of theirs which was described—that to mock the Prophet, they make faces and distort tones to turn words into something else, and they have considered this making of faces and distortion of words a skill—on the basis of that, they became deserving that their faces actually be distorted. By the same analogy, those who have made turning away from the truth their way—they are deserving that their faces be turned entirely toward the rear.

'wa kana amrullahi maf'ula' (And Allah's command is always executed) clarified the reality: let no one think that making men's faces flat like the backs of heads, or turning them around, or distorting them into the shape of apes is a difficult task for God. There is no distance between any command of His and its occurrence. On one side the command occurred, on the other its result is present.

'alam tara ilal-ladhina utu nashibam-minal-kitabi yu'minuna bil-jibti wat-taghuti wa yaquluna lil-ladhina kafaru ha'ula'i ahda minal-ladhina amanu sabila'.

Meaning of 'Jibt' and 'Taghut': 'Jibt' refers to base acts, such as magic, sorcery, spells, charms, geomancy, divination, astrology, walking on fire, and other such absurdities. Palmistry is also included in this.

In the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah under verses 286 and 288, we explained in detail that the Jews, in their era of decline, had put the Divine Book behind their backs and remained devoted only to these things. Their prophets lamented this state of theirs in extremely heart-rending words. The necessary references were quoted there. Repeating them here would be lengthy.

Detailed discussion on 'Taghut' passed under verse 256 of Al-Baqarah. 

Religion's Foundation is on Tawheed: The foundation of religion is on Tawheed. This is not just one creed among creeds but the establishment and survival of the entire religion depends upon it. Those who protect it from every aspect are the ones who, despite their other shortcomings, protect their original religion. In contrast, those who produce a loophole in Tawheed demolish the original religion. Consequently, their other works too—which are apparently of religiosity—remain entirely useless. For this reason, Allah Almighty will not forgive Shirk, but He will forgive other sins for whom He wills. The qualifier "for whom He wills" is proof that regarding other sins too, no one should be bold. This is because their forgiveness also depends on Allah's will. No one else has any role in His will, nor is any of His wills devoid of wisdom. Furthermore, being bold and daring regarding sins is itself a type of Shirk. 

Allah Almighty will not forgive Shirk: This preamble was stated to clarify the reality that the reason the Jews have become deserving of the curse is that, while being bearers of the Book, they uprooted the very foundation of the religion and adopted Shirk in its place. 'Shirk' is a great fabrication against Allah which Allah Almighty is never going to forgive. We clarified the reason for calling Shirk a fabrication elsewhere: those committing Shirk claim, to give their polytheistic acts the authority of religion, that Allah Almighty commanded them these points. Obviously, this is an explicit slander against Allah Almighty. And if a group commissioned to bear witness to Allah's religion adopts the profession of slandering God, what else could they deserve except the curse!

After this preamble, three types of their Shirk are counted here.

The Jews' Polytheistic Deeds and Creeds: One is that they consider themselves a superior and chosen group. Their thought is that they are the children of God's beloveds and are themselves God's beloveds and favorites; therefore, there is no accountability or punishment for them with God. Whatever their deeds and morals may be, first of all, they will not be cast into Hell; if they were, it would be only for a short duration. This arrogance has entirely absolved them of the responsibilities of action and obedience, and they have removed themselves from the circle of servitude to enter the category of divinity, even though Allah has nowhere granted them this certificate of being chosen; whoever is granted greatness, it is from God, and God has linked this thing with faith, action, goodness, and Taqwa rather than lineage and descent. Every person will pay for what they do. Allah is not one to commit even an atom's weight of injustice toward them. This creed of their superiority which they have fabricated is their own brainchild. Their attributing it to God is a false fabrication against God, and this crime alone is sufficient for them to be criminals, setting aside other crimes.

Base Acts and Evil Spirits: Second is that while being bearers of the Book, they believe in 'Jibt' and 'Taghut' and are believers in and practitioners of base acts. Regarding base acts, it should be remembered that they relate mostly to satanic powers and evil spirits. These are what are called 'Taghut' here. Those who pursue these acts, first of all, believe evil spirits to be effective in themselves; then, to establish a connection with them and use them for their purposes, they have to commit not only acts contrary to Sharia but explicitly polytheistic acts, by which creed and action are both ruined at once. Cast a glance at what has been written on this in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah too. 

Third is that these people support the disbelievers and polytheists against the believers and consider them more on the truth and guidance than the Muslims. This point has also passed in Al-Baqarah and Al Imran. 

Support for Polytheists against the Believers: The Jews had become so blind in opposing Islam that they openly preferred the polytheists of Makkah over the Muslims and made those teachings and concessions of Islam the cover for this opposition which were against their innovations or the extremisms of their Sharia. For example, in the state of Hadath and Janaba, when Islam gave permission for Tayammum in the case of water not being available, they made this too a means of mischief-making and started saying: "Can a religion that allows even performing prayer by striking hands on the ground in the state of Janaba be a Divine religion? The religion of these idol-worshippers is better than theirs." It should be remembered that regarding purification, the Jewish jurists had produced such extremisms that a person in the state of Janaba remained a complete untouchable. Let alone Janaba, it is known from the Gospel that the Jewish jurists used to object to Sayyidina Jesus's companions on the point that these people sometimes eat food without washing their hands. Sayyidina Jesus had likened them to 'whited sepulchres' for these types of hair-splittings of theirs: just as sepulchres are whited on the outside but contain rotting and decaying bones inside, similarly these people appear very bright and clean on the outside but are filled with the wealth of looting inside. This same mindset of the Jews manifested against the Muslims. They were ready to tolerate even the polytheists. They were not ready to tolerate the Muslims! Obviously, just as supporting the truth is truth-worship, supporting Shirk is Shirk-worship.

In this verse, it stated: these are the ones whom Allah has cursed, and for those whom Allah curses, no helper can be of any use to them. For one upon whom God's curse occurs, their root is gone from God's presence, and a tree whose root is cut—no matter how much water one gives it, its becoming green is impossible.

'am lahum nashibum-minal-mulki fa-idhal-la yu'tun-nasa naqira'. 'Mulk' (sovereignty) here refers to Divine authority and power, and 'An-Nas' (the people) here refers to the Muslims. 

The meaning is: do they have some partnership in God's authority and power such that they may grant a share from His favor and reward to whom they will and deprive whom they will, and consequently, on the basis of this authority of theirs, they want to keep the Muslims deprived of God's favor and kindness? If this is not the case, then what is gained from all this absurdity? Who has ever won by wrestling with Divine destiny, that they will win!

'am yahsudunan-nasa 'ala ma atahumullahu min fadhlihi'. The Jews were Envious of the Ishmaelites: In this verse, the original secret was unveiled: all this storm is the result of the envy they hold against the Muslims. They have this grief and rage: Prophethood was the portion of their family; how did it exit their family to go into the Ishmaelites? They do not know that Prophethood and Sharia are Allah's favor; Allah grants His favor to whom He wills. Envying the favor granted by Allah and rising for opposition in the crisis of this envy is equivalent to fighting Allah Himself. If they have risen to fight Allah, then let them fight; We have already granted the family of Abraham the Book and Wisdom and granted them a great kingdom. Meaning: let them do whatever they have to do; We have already done what We had to do. 

'faqad atayna ala Ibrahimal-kitaba wal-hikmata wa ataynahum-mulkan 'azhima'.

A Style of Language: In conditional and imperative sentences, when 'faqad' appears like this, something is omitted in the speech before it, the detail of which is provided by the subsequent sentence. Here the aim is: if these people are opposing this Prophet due to envy of the Ishmaelites, then let them envy as much as they want; We have already granted the family of Abraham the Book and Wisdom and a great kingship. 

'Al-e-Ibrahim' (Family of Abraham) refers to the Ishmaelites: Although 'Al-e-Ibrahim' is general, here the Ishmaelites are intended. Context is the proof for this. This is because this point is being said to the Children of Israel as a rebuke. For this reason, they cannot be included in it, and when they cannot be included, the only referent remains the Ishmaelites. Furthermore, the mention here is of granting the Book, Wisdom, and Caliphate, and this granting is after the curse upon the Children of Israel; therefore, the question of their inclusion does not even arise. Additionally, it is proven from the Torah itself that the Jews always attributed themselves to Sayyidina Isaac instead of Sayyidina Abraham. The Torah says that Abraham's offspring will be called by the name of Isaac. In contrast, the Arabs always attributed themselves to Sayyidina Abraham, because Sayyidina Abraham resided there, built Baitullah there, and performed all his rites there. 

From this style of expression, first, the point manifests: let the Children of Israel not think that the honor of being the 'Family of Abraham' belongs only to them. This honor also belongs to the Ishmaelites. Second, this is a hint toward that promise Allah Almighty made to Prophet Abraham, which related explicitly to Prophet Ishmael and his descendants. In the Torah, this promise is mentioned thus: 'And the angel of the LORD called unto Abraham out of heaven the second time, And said, By myself have I sworn, saith the LORD, for because thou hast done this thing, and hast not withheld thy son, thine only son: That in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed as the stars of the heaven, and as the sand which is upon the sea shore; and thy seed shall possess the gate of his enemies; And in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed; because thou hast obeyed my voice' (Genesis 22:15-18). From this description of the Torah, it is perfectly clear that Allah Almighty made this promise of blessing to Prophet Abraham at the time when he succeeded in the test of sacrificing his only son—Prophet Ishmael. For this reason, this promise can necessarily relate only to Prophet Ishmael and his lineage. 

Three Promises of Allah Almighty for the Ishmaelites: In this promise, three points are mentioned. One, that Allah Almighty will make them a great nation. Second, that they will achieve great victories and their possession will be over the gates of enemies. Third, that through this lineage, all the nations of the earth will be blessed. These three promises were fulfilled by the advent of the Holy Prophet (PBUH). From his advent, a great nation manifested; this nation became the owner of the gates of enemies; and through his call, the entire human world was blessed with the bounty of religion and Sharia. 

The mention of the Promise in the form of an Event: It is the practical manifestation of this promise to which the verse under discussion hints. Although at the time this verse was revealed, this promise had not fully manifested, the Divine decision for its fulfillment had been issued; therefore, it was expressed in a way as if it had practically been fulfilled. There are multiple examples of this style of expression in the Holy Quran; we present one example here: 'And [remember] when Moses said to his people, "O my people, remember the favor of Allah upon you when He appointed among you prophets and made you kings and gave you that which He had not given anyone among the worlds. O my people, enter the Holy Land which Allah has assigned to you and do not turn back [from fighting] and [thus] become losers."' (Al-Ma'idah: 20-21). Sayyidina Moses (PBUH) made this speech before the nation at the time he was inviting them to attack the Holy Land. Obviously, until that time, not a single one of these points had manifested, but the decision for these points had been made with Allah Almighty and He had informed Sayyidina Moses of this decision; for this reason, Sayyidina Moses mentioned them as if these promises had been fulfilled. 

Government is among the Fruits of the Book and Wisdom: From this verse, a subtle nuance is also clarified: that sovereignty and Caliphate are among the fruits and results of the Book and Wisdom. When Allah Almighty blesses a nation with the bounty of the Book and Wisdom and that nation accepts it with true gratitude, it means the position of Imamate and Caliphate has also been entrusted to it. This subject has been described in several places in the Quran, but here it is described with special care. Those with a taste for the Arabic language can understand that there is great eloquence in the repetition of the verb 'atayna' (We gave). The Jews' entire envy was precisely because they knew that the kingship of this earth was also bound with this Quran; consequently, to strike a heavy blow upon this envy of theirs, He stated: We gave them not only the Book and Wisdom, but along with that, We gave them a great kingdom too. Despite your envy!

Warning to the Ishmaelites: 'fa-minhum man amana bihi wa minhum man sadda 'anhu wa kafa bi-jahannama sa'ira. innal-ladhina kafaru bi-ayatina sawfa nushlihim nara kullama nadhijat juluduhum baddalnahum juludan ghayraha liyadhuqul-'adhab innallaha kana 'azizun hakima'. These verses (55 to 58) relate to the Ishmaelites. It stated: one group among them has already been honored with faith by accepting this Book and Wisdom, but one group is still turning away from it. Regarding this group, it was stated: if it persists in its Kufr, Allah Almighty will cast it into Hell where there will be no reduction in their punishment. Whenever their skins are cooked, they will be given other skins so that their punishment remains fresh. Allah is Mighty (Aziz), meaning dominant—no one can catch His hand. He is Wise (Hakim), meaning none of His actions is devoid of justice and wisdom.

Regarding the second group who believed, it was stated: We will admit them into gardens in which they will remain forever and in which there will be pure spouses for them. The clarification of all these components passed in Surah Al-Baqarah. Wherever the Quran has mentioned its great favor upon the Ishmaelites, it has certainly clarified that this favor relates to faith and Islam. It is not merely based on family and lineage. Among the Ishmaelites too, only those are partners in this Divine reward who believe in this Quran and this Prophet; those who do not believe will all go to Hell, whether Israelite or Ishmaelite. In Surah Al-Jumu'ah: 'It is He who has sent among the unlettered a Messenger from themselves reciting to them His verses and purifying them and teaching them the Book and wisdom - although they were before in clear error - And [to] others of them who have not yet joined them. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise.' Here too, in the final segment, there is a hint toward the disbelievers of the Quraysh who had not yet been included among those accepting this bounty, and the words carry an element of warning. This warning is precisely so that the Ishmaelites remain aware of the reality: Allah has performed a very great favor upon them, but this favor is only for those who value it; it will not be obtained merely on the basis that they are from the Ishmaelites. Since the Jews were deprived of Allah Almighty's favor by falling into this same type of misconception, the Quran delivered this awareness to the Ishmaelites in the very first stage.

Subject of Verses 58 to 70: Further on, addressing the Muslims, they are first advised: this trust of the Divine Sharia being snatched from the Jews and handed over to you—do not fall into the disease of national and group prejudice like the Jews, but always keep truth and justice in sight. Now you are being made heirs to a great kingdom along with the Book and Wisdom, and the responsibility for people's affairs is being placed upon you; so fulfill people's rights and always respect justice in your decisions, and remember that the God who commissioned you for this responsibility is All-Hearing and All-Seeing.

Following this, the method is stated by adopting which Muslims can remain organized, stable, established on truth and justice, and protected from the calamities arising from dispute and discord as the Muslim Ummah. This is effectively the detail of the foundations upon which the Islamic system of government is based. Following this, attention was turned toward those who had joined the Muslims but whose loyalty was still divided; they were not yet fully satisfied with the obedience of Allah, the Messenger, and the 'Ulul-Amr' (those in authority) of the Ummah. Since the primary loophole in the Ummah's unity and the Islamic government was feared to arise from them, attention was turned toward them in detail. In this light, recite the following verses.

'innallaha ya'murukum an tu'addul-amanati ila ahliha wa idha hakamtum baynan-nasi an tahkumu bil-'adli innallaha ni'imma ya'izhukum bihi innallaha kana sami'am-bashira'.

The word 'Amanat' (Trust) in its Broad Meaning: The word 'Amanat' here is not in its limited meaning but is used in an extremely broad sense, just as in the verse: 'Indeed, we offered the Trust [al-amanah] to the heavens and the earth and the mountains...'. All rights and duties—whether relating to the rights of Allah or the rights of servants, whether of an individual nature or a collective nature, whether relating to one's own or to strangers, whether of the type of financial transactions or political treaties, whether of the era of peace or of war. In short, whatever nature and degree the rights and duties are, they all enter into the meaning of 'Amanat'. And after entrusting the trust of Sharia and authority to the Muslims, the first instruction given collectively was: fulfill correctly those rights and duties for which you are being made responsible.

The Right of the Trust: If the previous contents of this Surah are preserved in the mind, there can be no difficulty in understanding that within this instruction is also hidden the allusion: those from whom this trust is being snatched and given to you—they did not fulfill its right. They concealed the position of testimony for which they were commissioned; they altered the Book placed in their custody; they created difference in the Sharia they were made to bear; they betrayed the rights of which they were made trustees; they proved to be thieves in the duties entrusted to them; they broke all the covenants they bound. For this reason, your primary responsibility is to correctly fulfill those rights and duties of which you are now being made bearers in the form of this great trust. Using the word 'Amanat' for rights and duties, first, produces the concept that these are all trusts entrusted by God. Therefore, their imposer is God alone. Second, that regarding all these trusts, an accounting must necessarily occur one day on behalf of the Entruster; if any betrayal occurs in them, there is no one who can save from God's grasp.

'wa idha hakamtum baynan-nasi an tahkumu bil-'adli'. The Most Important Aspect of the Trust: This is both the detail of the most important aspect of the trust and the clarification of the responsibility associated with authority. Those whom Allah Almighty blesses with authority in His earth—the primary responsibility that falls upon them is precisely this: that they settle the disputes arising among people with justice and fairness. Justice means that in the eyes of the law, there is no difference between rich and poor, noble and base, or black and white; fairness should not become a buyable and sellable thing; no partiality, no prejudice, and no laxity should find a way into it. No pressure, no force/influence, and no fear or greed should be given the opportunity to affect it. Those whom Allah Almighty blesses with authority in this earth—He blesses them for this very justice. For this reason, the greatest responsibility is for this thing. With God, the reward of a just ruler is very great, and the punishment of an unjust one is very severe. For this reason, the warning was given: this is an extremely high advice Allah Almighty is giving you; let there be no shortcoming in it. Finally, He referenced His attributes of 'Sami'' and 'Basir': remember that God hears and sees everything; even the most hidden injustice is not going to remain hidden from Him.

'ya-ayyu-hal-ladhina amanu ati'ullaha wa ati'ur-rasula wa ulil-amri minkum fa-in tanaza'tum fi shay'in farudduhu ilallahi war-rasuli in kuntum tu'minuna billahi wal-yawmil-akhiri dhalika khayruw-wa ahsanu ta'wila'.

Meaning of 'Ulul-Amr': 'Ulul-Amr' refers to the 'Arbab-e-Hall o 'Aqd' (those who loose and bind/decision-makers), responsible persons, and heads of the Islamic society. Depending on the society's circumstances, its referents can be both the people of knowledge and insight and the people of authority and politics. Whoever is in a position to lead the masses is a referent of this word. If an Imam/Caliph is present, then he and his officials are the 'Ulul-Amr'; and if these are not present, then those within the community who possess understanding of affairs and insight are intended by it. In another place in this Surah, this word is used: 'And when there comes to them information about [public] security or fear, they spread it. But if they had referred it back to the Messenger or to those of authority [ulil-amr] among them, those who can draw correct conclusions [yastanbitunahu] from it would have known it.'

Distinguishing Characteristic of the 'Ulul-Amr': In the era this verse was revealed, obviously neither the Caliphate existed nor were there formal 'Amirs' and officials. For this reason, 'Ulul-Amr' would refer to those among the Companions who possessed deep understanding of religious and social affairs and were the people's reference point for trust. Here, the word 'istinbat' (deduction/inference) expresses the distinguishing characteristic of the 'Ulul-Amr', from which the point emerges that in Islam, the position of religious and political leadership is originally for those who possess the capability of insight and Ijtihad. Class, brotherhood, family, and property, etc., have no role in it.

Meaning of 'Ta'wil': We discussed the word 'Ta'wil' under verse 7 of Surah Al Imran. 'Ala ya'ulu awlan wa ma'alan' means to return or refer to something. From this comes the word 'Ta'wil', which means to return a point to its original source and referent. From this meaning, it began to be used for the interpretation of a dream, the reality of a point, and the explanation and clarification of a speech, because in these forms too, the point is returned to its original source and objective. In the verse under discussion, 'ahsanu ta'wila' means that in disputed issues, referring to the word of Allah and the Messenger is better in terms of both reaching the truth and the final end. God's knowledge alone is the referent of all knowledge and reality, His Being alone is the refuge for all, and He alone possesses true sovereignty.

Three Centers of Command and Obedience in Islam: Every collective and political system is formed by command (amr) and obedience (ta'ah). In Islam, the centers of command and obedience are three: Allah, the Messenger, and the 'Ulul-Amr'. Among these, the first two mentioned are independent and intrinsic centers of obedience. For this reason, the verb 'ati'u' (obey) was used independently with them. The obedience of the 'Ulul-Amr' is under the obedience of Allah and the Messenger; for this reason, the verb 'ati'u' was not used separately for them but they were merely conjoined to the previous ones. This is a hint toward the fact that the 'Ulul-Amr' are only a means for the implementation of the commands of Allah and the Messenger; therefore, their obedience is not permissible against the commands of God and the Messenger. (Note that obedience to someone's command against the commands of Allah and the Messenger is not permissible, but regarding the 'Amirs' and officials, some details are also described with this command of the Sharia which are extremely necessary to know. We discussed this issue in detail in the chapter on the limits and conditions of obedience in our book 'The Islamic State').

'fa-in tanaza'tum fi shay'in': 'nazara fi-sh-shay'', 'tanaza'a fil-hadith', 'tanaza'a fil-amr' means—as we clarified in the exegesis of Surah Al Imran—difference of opinion; meaning in some matter, someone's opinion is one thing and another's is another. Context proves that here it refers to that difference of opinion which can occur in the chapter of determining a command of the Sharia in some matter. For example, a difference of opinion occurs in the expression and interpretation of a Shari text (nass), or a difference arises in an Ijtihadi matter. This difference can occur in the interpretation of a verse of the Quran or a Sunnah of the Prophet, and also in determining what is 'most in accord' (awfaq) with the Book and Sunnah in non-textual transactions. By the same analogy, this can occur between the 'Ulul-Amr' and the masses, and also among the 'Ulul-Amr' themselves. When such a difference occurs, the Ummah was instructed for its solution to refer that matter to Allah and the Messenger. Referring to Allah and the Messenger means that if no certain guidance exists for that matter in the texts of the Book and Sunnah, then by keeping their hints, requirements, context, and parallels and examples in view, determine what is 'most in accord' with the Book and Sunnah in it and adopt it. It stated: this method is the best and highest from the aspect of 'Ta'wil' (result/interpretation). This is because the dominant presumption is that this point will be in accord with the word of Allah and the Messenger, and the decision of the difference will be according to that law which is the original law and the center and referent of all Fiqh and Ijtihad in Islam; and this very method is that of holding fast to Divine sovereignty in the collective and political system with full strength and of 'I'tisam bi-Hablillah' (holding fast to the Rope of Allah), and this is true Tawheed.

Consensus (Ijma') is the Textual Method for Removing Difference: It should also be noted here that this instruction was given to the Ummah in its capacity as an Ummah. In such instructions, although the address is general, the responsibility for their practical implementation falls upon the 'Arbab-e-Hall o 'Aqd' of the Ummah—or in the Quran's words, the 'Ulul-Amr'. For this reason, it is their responsibility that if a difference of opinion occurs in any matter, they refer to the original law of Sharia—i.e., the Book and Sunnah—and adopt the point that appears most in accord with it. The agreement of the 'Arbab-e-Hall o 'Aqd' or their majority, under the guidance of the 'Sahib-e-Amr' (the one in authority, i.e., the Caliph and Imam), on some matter being 'most in accord' with the Sharia is called 'Ijma'' (Consensus) in the Sharia, which is a textual method for removing difference, and its opposition is not permissible for anyone.

Book, Sunnah, and Ijtihad: The method of 'referring to Allah and the Messenger' is that when one wants to know the Sharia's command in any matter, they first refer to the Book of Allah. If it is not found there, they refer to the Prophet's Sunnah. If it is not found there either, then the path to knowing it is Ijtihad. The etiquettes and conditions of Ijtihad known from the Prophet's (PBUH) teaching and the Companions' practice are present in the books of 'Usul al-Fiqh' (Principles of Jurisprudence) and are so natural and rational that no reasonable person has room for their denial.

Like the Book of Allah, the Status of the Sunnah is also Permanent: From this verse, it is also known that as the referent of Islamic law, the status of the Prophet's Sunnah is independent and permanent, just like the Book of Allah. This is why it stated: 'fa-rudduhu ilallahi war-rasul' (so refer it to Allah and the Messenger).
