Obviously, this instruction cannot be limited only to the Prophet's (PBUH) blessed life, because the dominant possibility of this difference arising was precisely after the Prophet's passing, and the verse itself is testifying that its connection is with the future. Obviously, after the Prophet's (PBUH) passing, it is only his Sunnah that can be his substitute. There is no room here to accept that the 'Ulul-Amr' of the time can be the Prophet's (PBUH) substitute, because the 'Ulul-Amr' were omitted here [in the referral], which is clear proof that the 'Ulul-Amr' hold no independent status in religion as a referent of the law. Independent status belongs only to Allah and the Messenger, and to the Messenger also because Allah Almighty commissioned him for the teaching and clarification of His law and protected him from error and sin for the correct performance of this position's responsibilities. Effectively, true sovereignty belongs only to Allah Almighty; the Messenger is merely an infallible means for telling Allah's commands and His pleasures.

'tahakum ilal-hakim' means 'takhashum ilayh'—meaning he presented his dispute and matter before the judge. The research of 'Taghut' passed in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah. Here, opposite it, He stated: 'Come to what Allah has revealed and to the Messenger,' from which the point emerges that 'Taghut' here is used in the meaning of the opposite of the Book of Allah and the Messenger. Obviously, in Madinah and its surroundings until the era of the verse's revelation, this status was held only by the chiefs and leaders of the Jews: that those who wanted to escape the Prophet's (PBUH) court would take their matters to them; for this reason, only they can be intended by 'Taghut', and from every aspect, they were the perfectly correct referents of this word.

'alam tara ilal-ladhina yaz'umuna annahum amanu bi-ma unzila ilayka wa ma unzila min qablika yuriduna ay-yatahakamu itat-taghut'. This mentions the hypocrites in a tone of wonder (and from the clues it is clearly known that these hypocrites were people who had come from the People of the Book) who claimed that they believe in the Quran and also believe in the previous scriptures, yet in their matters, instead of referring to Allah and the Messenger, they referred to the chiefs of the Jews and their courts. Even though from that Allah and Messenger in whom they claimed faith, this clear instruction had already been revealed: for faith in Allah and the Messenger, it is mandatory that 'Taghut' be denied; without this denial, faith is not authoritative. But they wanted to gather both, and Satan wants to thus mislead them and put them so far from the straight path that no possibility remains for them to accept it. 

'wa qad umiru ay-yakfuru bihi wa yuridu-sh-shaytanu ay-yudhillahum dhalalam-ba'ida'.

Threat to the Hypocrites: 'wa idha qila lahum ta'alaw ila ma anzalallahu wa ilar-rasuli ra'aytal-munafiqina yasudduna 'anka shududa'. It stated: today when they are told to present their matters before Allah and the Messenger alone—as this is the demand of faith—they evade through some pretext or another; but what will happen when, in retribution for these mischiefs of theirs, such a time comes upon them that they will come running to you and swear oaths to assure you that whatever they have been doing, they have not been doing with any bad intention but with the intention of goodness and compatibility.

'fa-kayfa idha ashabathum-mushibatum-bi-ma qaddamat aydihim': The disaster (mushibah) whose occurrence is mentioned here happened later in this way: when Islam gained strength and the Jews' political power became entirely weak, the Muslims were instructed to change the method of turning a blind eye and ignoring regarding the hypocrites. Consequently, the Muslims changed their method and began the accountability of the hypocrites at every step. The hypocrites were very distressed by this situation. Neither did enough strength remain in the Jews to patronize them, nor were the Muslims now ready to fall for their tricks. 'Neither a place to stay, nor a foot to go.'

'thumma ja'uka yahlifuna billahi in aradna illa ihsanaw-wa tawfiqa'. The Hypocrites' Conduct: The result was that the hypocrites would come running to the Holy Prophet's (PBUH) presence and try to assure you by swearing oaths: that the link and connection they maintained with the Jews until now and the way they sometimes accepted their superiority in their matters and cases—no corruption of intention had a role in it; rather, their desire was only that Muslims would benefit from it and the gulf of difference and enmity standing between the Jews and Muslims would not become more vast. In this way, they tried to hide their hypocrisy behind the veil of reconciliation and described it with the beautiful words of 'Ihsan' (goodness) and 'Tawfiq' (compatibility). But after the events had been 'exposed from the roof' (become public), the opportunity for this word-making had entirely departed; consequently, further on in this Surah and then in detail in Surah At-Tawbah, this mask on the hypocrites' faces was ripped off and thrown away, and it became impossible for them to hide their faces. 

A Political Point: From this, the point emerged that making a policy of difference or tolerance with rival powers is the work of the 'Arbab-e-Hall o 'Aqd' of the Ummah and its heads, not of some clique of the masses. If the 'Arbab-e-Hall o 'Aqd' are at war with a rival power and some individuals from among the masses extend the swings of love and trust toward them and call it well-wishing for the Ummah and an effort for mutual compatibility, then this is explicit ill-will and open hypocrisy.

The Original Secret of the Hypocrites' Hearts: It stated: 'ula'ikal-ladhina ya'lamullahu ma fi qulubihim' (Those are the ones of whom Allah knows what is in their hearts). Allah Almighty well knows what is in their hearts. Is it the passion for well-wishing for the Ummah and creating compatibility that is making them 'bake these papads' (go through these troubles), or is it the corruption of hypocrisy and 'Taghut-worship' that is not yet allowing them to become single-minded toward Islam, and they are nurturing in the heart the hope that perhaps the result of this struggle will be in the form of the Jews' and disbelievers' victory, so this policy of hypocrisy of theirs will be successful? It stated: 'fa-a'ridh 'anhum wa 'izh-hum wa qul-lahum fi anfusihim qawlam-baligha'. Turn away from this act of theirs, explain good and evil to them well, and inform them of what is better for them in such a style that their ears open and the point enters their hearts.

The threat in 'fa-kayfa idha ashabathum mushibah...' has become even more sharp and fierce in 'fa-a'ridh 'anhum wa 'izh-hum wa qul-lahum fi anfusihim qawlam-baligha'. The meaning is: God is well-aware of the reality of the excuse-making they are doing for this hypocrisy of theirs; for this reason, you refer the matter to God and turn away from them for now; however, explain good and evil to them well—that the game they are playing will prove extremely dangerous for themselves in the future. Regarding the word 'wa'zh' (admonition), we have written elsewhere that in Arabic this word also carries the meaning of rebuke and warning.

Eloquence of 'qawlam-baligha': In 'qul-lahum fi anfusihim qawlam-baligha', the words 'fi anfusihim' (regarding themselves) express the reality that it be made clear to them: this advice is better for their own selves; Islam is not going to suffer any harm from this way of theirs; Allah is sufficient for the protection of His religion. However, they will destroy themselves. The words 'qawlam-baligha' (a reaching/effective word) express the reality that the style of advice should now be ear-opening and heart-penetrating. These are deaf and dull people; therefore, a noble style of advice does not affect them.

The Prophet (PBUH) received this instruction because if you (PBUH) took note of someone's error, you would point toward it in an extremely soft and noble manner due to the highest degree of kindness and compassion. Although the style befitting your (PBUH) status was this, and for capable people this hint was sufficient, the hypocrites were neither worthy nor appreciative of this noble nature; rather, they took wrong advantage of it and were becoming bolder in their mischiefs day by day. For this reason, the instruction was given that now is not the time to practice much softness with them, but the time has come to warn them in clear words and inform them well of their good and evil so that they may recover before falling into the pit of destruction, if they wish to recover.

Exegesis of Verses 64 to 65:
The Original Status of a Messenger: Now the correct status of a Messenger was clarified: a Messenger is not just to be believed in, but he comes so that he may be obeyed. He is not just a center of devotion but also a center of obedience; his status is not just that of a preacher and advisor but that of a mandatory-to-obey guide. Allah Almighty commissions him under His permission so that people obey his commands in all matters. This is because his obedience is indirectly the obedience of God. Those who claim to believe in the Messenger but do not accept his political authority or want to keep themselves free from it—their claim of faith is false.

Here the qualifier "bi-idhnillah" (by Allah's leave) expresses the reality that the original sovereignty belongs to Allah, but He grants this position to His Messenger by His permission so that he informs people of His command and prohibition, and for this purpose, He protects him from error and sin; for this reason, the Messenger is the manifestation of God's legal and legislative sovereignty, and faith in him—along with his unhesitating obedience—becomes synonymous with faith in God and the obedience of God. 

The Messenger is the Manifestation of God's Legislative Sovereignty: Obviously, when the Messenger is the manifestation of God's legal and legislative sovereignty, no room remains for any possessor of faith to leave the Messenger's court and take any of their matters for decision to the court of 'Taghut'. The person who does so wrongs their own soul because in reality, this thing is a denial of God's sovereignty and the commission of indirect Shirk and Kufr. Consequently, regarding those hypocrites who took their matters to the courts of the Jews, it was stated: for those who wronged their own souls, the only form for its reform and for salvation from its consequences was that they attend the Prophet's presence, admit their error, seek forgiveness from God, and the Messenger also intercede for them through a prayer for forgiveness; then Allah Almighty would accept their repentance and have mercy on them. Apart from this, there is no other form for its amends. 

Fundamental Condition of Faith: Obedience to the Messenger Outwardly and Inwardly: Following this, Allah Almighty swore by His own Being: these people cannot be believers until they make you (PBUH) the judge in all disputes arising among them and then, simultaneously, a mental change occurs within them such that they accept your decision without hesitation with full peace of heart and surrender themselves to you without any exception or reservation. The obedience of the Messenger is synonymous with the obedience of God Himself; for this reason, its right is not fulfilled merely by outward obedience, but the obedience of the heart is a condition for it. Here, the occasion and place of the oath "fa-la wa rabbika" (But no, by your Lord) should also be noted. It is not only intended to emphasize the outward and inward obedience of the Messenger, but it is also the refutation of the hypocrites' false oath (mentioned in verse 62) with a true oath. Then, in the address of 'wa rabbika' (by your Lord), the heart-pleasing nature of the special digression (Iltifat) from Allah Almighty for the Prophet (PBUH) is such that only those with taste can estimate its eloquences; the pen is incapable of their expression.

The Prophet's Seeking Forgiveness is equivalent to Intercession: In 'fastagh-farullaha wastagh-fara lahumur-rasul', the condition of the Messenger's seeking forgiveness placed for them has two aspects. One is that this seeking of forgiveness by the Messenger is equivalent to intercession for them in this world, from which the expectation of this great sin being forgiven is held; second, that in the presence of the Messenger's court, their 'tahakum itat-taghut' (seeking judgment from Taghut) is an explicit insult to the Messenger; therefore, it became necessary that they also obtain the Messenger's pleasure and his prayer. If the hypocrites had thought, they had a great opportunity to benefit from the Prophet's blessings, but many among them did not value this opportunity, the result of which was that Allah Almighty later deprived them of it. In Surah Al-Munafiqun, its mention is like this: 'And when it is said to them, "Come, the Messenger of Allah will ask forgiveness for you," they turn their heads aside and you see them evading while they are arrogant. It is all the same for them whether you ask forgiveness for them or do not ask forgiveness for them; never will Allah forgive them. Indeed, Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people' (Al-Munafiqun: 5-6).

Exegesis of Verses 66 to 70:
'wa law anna katabna 'alayhim aniqtulu anfusakum awikhruju min diyarikum ma fa'aluhu illa qalilum-minhum wa law annahum fa'alu ma yu'azhuna bihi lakana khayral-lahum wa ashaddu tathbita'.

The Fundamental Weakness of the Hypocrites: Now the original cause of hypocrisy is unveiled: these people have made themselves claimants of Islam, but they are not yet fully free from the snares of previous links and relationships of the era of ignorance; the chains of family, brotherhood, tribe, and nation are still on their feet, and the attachments of homeland and territory are also clinging to them; for this reason, instead of moving forward, they are repeatedly looking back, even though the first demand of Islam is that a person break every chain and rise only for Allah, just as the first Emigrants (Muhajirun) rose.

The Original Demand of Friendship with Truth: It should be noted that these hypocrites were mostly from the Jews and the tribes around Madinah. Seeing the rising power of Islam, they had been forced into the expression of Islam, but—as passed above—they also wanted to maintain their links with the Jews and their tribal chiefs, and for this purpose, they referred to them in their matters and cases too. The Quran unveiled this weakness of theirs: if they were commanded to fight their own people and leave their homes to join the Muslims, very few among them would be ready for this Jihad and migration. Regarding the meaning of "iqtulu anfusakum" (kill yourselves/each other), cast a glance at what we wrote under Al-Baqarah verse 54 and An-Nisa verse 69. Jihad is first of all a hard trial by its nature, but when this sword has to be raised against those with whom there are bonds of blood and kinship and whose spirit of zeal and support is running through the veins and fibers, then this trial becomes even harder, because in this form the sword effectively has to be run over one's own necks. But Islam gives no importance to blood and lineage in comparison to the truth. For this reason, to pass the test of God's loyalty, the believers have to pass through this stage too. Consequently, the history of Islam is a witness that at the occasion of Badr, the uncle's sword was unsheathed against the nephew and the nephew's against the uncle, and all links of the zeal of ignorance became entirely reality-less before the truth. Toward this same reality of Islam, these hypocrites are also being directed here: if they too become free and single-minded from the attachments of family, tribe, and home and join the society of Muslims completely, it is better for them, and this thing will be extremely effective in anchoring their feet upon Islam. Coming out of the corrupt environment and reaching the pure environment, their weaknesses will be removed and they too will become God's loyal and truth's serving ones along with the life-risking ones of Islam.

The Blessings of Migration and Jihad: Following this, for their encouragement, it stated: do not think that this is some path of destruction and suicide. If they leave their homes for Allah, Allah will give them a great reward from His own presence and will bless them with guidance to the straight path. Those who cut off from everything to rise for the obedience of Allah and His Messenger obtain the companionship and alliance of Allah's rewarded servants—the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous; and how fortunate are those who obtain the companionship and alliance of this pure group! This is Allah's favor, and Allah is not unaware of the servants deserving of this special favor. Those who play the gambles of migration and Jihad to obtain this favor should be at peace: Allah is well-aware of their life-risking devotions.

Subject of Verses 71 to 76: The same subject as above is continuing. Although the address is to the general Muslims, the commentary is on the attitude of those hypocrites regarding whom it was stated above that they claim Islam but are not ready to suffer any blow in this path or pass through the trials of migration and Jihad. First, the Muslims were commanded to be armed for Jihad and to rise for war. It stated: if the need arises to rise for war as a collective community, then rise in a collective form; and if there is an opportunity to go out in the form of squads and contingents, then go out in the form of squads and contingents and perform Jihad in the path of Allah. After that, the hypocrites were mentioned: these people are themselves cowardly and also try to make others cowardly. Their state is that if some harm reaches you, they rejoice: "How good it was that we were not present with this army or squad"; and if you achieve some success, they say enviously: "If only we too were with this army or squad so that we could have been partners in the spoils obtained by it." After that, to urge toward Jihad, its great reward was also mentioned, and simultaneously, in extremely effective words, attention was also drawn toward that need which was its driver at that time: that need was that in various places, Muslim men, women, and children were surrounded in the snare of the disbelievers, being targets of various types of oppressions at their hands for the crime of entering Islam, and were making pleas to obtain release from their grasp of oppression. Rescuing them from this oppression and torture was a great human and Islamic duty. Then the difference between the Muslims' Jihad and the disbelievers' war was clarified: the Muslims' Jihad is in the path of Allah, and the disbelievers' war is in the path of Taghut. No matter how many tricks Satan plays, every trick of his will prove weak against God. The final success will nonetheless be obtained by those who rise to support the religion of Allah. In this light, recite the following verses.

71 to 73: 'ya-ayyu-hal-ladhina amanu khudhu hidhrakum fanfiru thubatin awinfiru jami'a. wa inna minkum lamay-yubatti'anna fa-in ashabatkum mushibatum-qala qad an'amallahu 'alayya idh lam akum-ma'ahum shahida'. 'Hidhr' originally means to protect oneself from a danger or calamity. Progressing from this meaning, this word was used for those things used in war to protect oneself from the enemy's attacks—for example, a coat of mail, a shield, a helmet, etc. Its specific usage is indeed for defensive equipment, but in its general usage, it is also spoken for those weapons used for attack—for example, arrows, guns, swords, etc. Here this word seems to be in its general meaning. Further discussion on this is coming under verse 102 of this Surah. There, the Quran itself has clarified both its general and specific usages. 'Thubat' is the plural of 'Thubah'. 'Thubah' means a group of riders, a squad, or a contingent. Two methods of war were well-known in Arabia. One, army-formation in the form of an organized army; second, the method adopted in guerrilla war—meaning raiding the enemy in the form of squads and contingents. Through the word 'Thubat', the hint is toward this very method. The Muslims used both methods. The Holy Prophet (PBUH) both led organized armies and sent 'Saraya' (expeditions) from time to time. 'Batta' yubatti'' means to become lax, to become sluggish, and to remain behind, and also to make others sluggish. In Lisan al-Arab: 'batta falanun bi-falanin idha thabbatahu' (so-and-so made so-and-so sluggish and low-spirited). In a Hadith: 'man batta bihi 'amaluhu lam yusri' bihi nasabuhu' (one whose deed makes them slow, their lineage will not make them fast).

This is a command to the Muslims as a collective to be armed and rise for war. It stated: if the turn arises to raid the enemy in the form of contingents and groups, then go out for that too; and if the need arises for an army-march in an organized collective form, then do not shrink from that either. 

The Hypocrites' Cowardice and their Envy at the Muslims' Success: Then it stated: 'wa la'in ashabakum fadhlam-minallahi layaqulanna ka-al-lam takum-baynakum wa baynahu mawaddatuy-ya-laytani kuntu ma'ahum fa-afu-za fawzan 'azhima'. There are some people among you who themselves shrink from war and also make others low-spirited. Their state is that if some harm reaches you in an expedition, they rejoice: "Allah saved us well that we were not partners with them in this expedition"; and if success is achieved by you, they say enviously: "If only we too were among them so that we could have obtained much spoils." Along with this second point, the words 'as if no affection existed between you and him' are casting a reflection on the interior of those saying this. The meaning is: if they do not participate in an expedition due to their wretched deeds, the minimum demand of faith-based and Islamic brotherhood is that they be happy at the Muslims' success—that Allah made their religious brothers successful; but they feel no happiness at this; rather, just as a person's heart burns at some success of a rival because they could not be a partner in it, similarly these people consider it not their success but the rival's success and beat their heads at their deprivation. As if they have no relationship with Islam and the Muslims.

'fal-yuqatil fi sabilillahi-l-ladhina yashtarunal-hayatad-dunya bil-akhirah wa may-yuqatil fi sabilillahi fa-yuqtal aw yaghlib fa-sawfa nu'tihi ajran 'azhima'.
'Alladhina' (Those who) is here not in the place of the object but in the status of the subject (fa'il), and 'shara yashri' here is in the meaning of 'selling.' In Surah Yusuf: 'wa sharawhu bi-thamanim-bakhsin darahima ma'dudah' (And they sold him for a reduced price - a few dirhams - and they were unappreciative of him). They sell the life of the world for the Hereafter—meaning they give preference to the Hereafter over the life of the world. The meaning of the verse is: these hypocrites only want to be the 'Ghazis' (warriors) of that war in which not even a scratch occurs and spoils also come into hand in abundance. God's religion is not in need of such so-called Ghazis. For Jihad in the path of God, let those rise who have already sacrificed their world for the Hereafter; those who perform Jihad for the success of the Hereafter alone by sacrificing the world—whether they are killed or victorious—in both forms there is a great reward for them. As for these people who have become 'Majnuns' (lovers) only for as long as a cup of milk is received from Layla's side and no demand for the heart's blood is made—such Majnuns are not required here.

'wa ma lakum la tuqatiluna fi sabilillahi wal-mustadh'afina minar-rijali wan-nisa'i wal-wildanil-ladhina yaquluna rabbana akhrijna min hadhihil-qaryatizh-zhalimi ahluha waj'al-lana mil-ladunka waliyyaw-waj'al-lana mil-ladunka nashira'.

The style of 'wa ma lakum' (And what is the matter with you) is for urging and motivating toward a task. 'Mustadh'af' refers to the oppressed, the compelled, and the helpless. The conjunction of 'mustadh'afina' with 'fi sabilillah' expresses that the war for the liberation of those being persecuted because of religion holds the first rank in 'fighting in the path of Allah.' We clarified in the exegesis of Al-Baqarah that the original objective of Islamic Jihad is to erase Fitna from the world. There is no reason to specify "Qaryah" (city/town) here only for Makkah. In the era of these verses' revelation, there were other settlements besides Makkah in which many men, women, and children had entered Islam and were targets of various types of oppressions at the hands of their disbelieving guardians or the disbelieving powerful ones of their tribe. The usage of 'min ladunka' (from Yourself) expresses that the manifest circumstances are entirely contrary, no ray of hope is visible from any side, but if Allah Almighty opens some path through His power and His favor, it is not far-fetched. The meaning is: why do you not rise to rescue those men, women, and children from the disbelievers' oppression and torture who are surrounded in a state of helplessness among the disbelievers and are finding no path to escape from them and join the Muslims—whose state is that day and night they are making this prayer with extreme restlessness: "O Lord! Take us out of this city of oppressive inhabitants and create for us from the unseen our sympathizers and raise for us from the unseen our helpers."

Hints of Verse 75: Several points manifest from this verse. One, that the oppressive disbelievers had made the land of their own homeland so tight for the weak Muslims that that homeland was 'biting' them, and despite the love for homeland being a natural thing, they were so disgusted with it that they called it the 'city of oppressive inhabitants' and were not ready to tolerate any kind of attribution to it for themselves. Second, that any homeland holds the status of a homeland for the believers only as long as there is peace for their religion and faith within it. If religion and faith do not have peace in it, then it is not a homeland but a den of bloodthirsty beasts, a dwelling of snakes and pythons, and a center of devils. Third, that in that era, circumstances were so hopeless that the oppressed Muslims were not even being shown any path of salvation in the manifest. All reliance was only on Allah's help—that He alone might open some path from the unseen. Despite this, these Muslims remained firm on their faith. Allahu Akbar! What a glory their steadfastness had! Even mountains cannot compete with this steadfastness. Fourth, that if Muslims are surrounded in such a state of oppression anywhere, then Jihad becomes mandatory upon all those Muslims who are in a position to help them. If they do not rise for their help, this is explicit hypocrisy.

'al-ladhina amanu yuqatiluna fi sabilillahi wal-ladhina kafaru yuqatiluna fi sabil-it-taghuti fa-qatilu awliya'ash-shaytani inna kaydash-shaytani kana dha'ifa'.

Discussion on the word 'Taghut' passed in the exegesis of both Al-Baqarah and Al Imran. Here the Quran itself clarified by saying 'fa-qatilu awliya'ash-shaytan' (so fight the allies of Satan) that 'Taghut' refers to Satan.

This verse is for the encouragement of the believers. The meaning is: the believers' war is in the path of God, and God is over their heads. In contrast, the disbelievers' war is in the path of Satan, and Satan is over their heads. Effectively, the encounter is originally between the Rahman (Merciful) and Satan. Since God has given Satan too a respite within a limited circle, he tells and teaches some tricks to his supporters; but after all, what can his allies do against God's 'firm plan' (kayd-e-matin)? For this reason, the believers should not fear them. If they have shown no weakness in God's loyalty, then success is nonetheless theirs. The natural causes of weakness hidden in Satan's tricks are for another suitable place of discussion. Here just remember that no firm foundation for any work can be established until that foundation is on the Truth. Since the foundation of every work of Satan is on falsehood, the question of its being stable does not even arise.

Subject of Verses 77 to 85: Further on, more weaknesses and mischiefs of those hypocrites are being clarified so that Muslims become aware of their Fitnas and so that the wrong and anti-Tawheed/Islam tendencies that could emerge within Muslims from their whisperings are thoroughly removed. First, attention was drawn toward this strange attitude of these hypocrites: until now, they were demanding Jihad very loudly to impose the awe of their faith and sincerity; it seemed as if every single one of them was intoxicated with the love of Jihad; but from the Prophet's side, they were instructed in patience and waiting—that its time has not yet come; for now, strengthen and organize yourselves through the observance of prayer and zakat so that when the time comes, you can fight in God's path with full believer-like glory of steadfastness. But now when the command for war has been given, they are hiding and they fear humans more than God and are complaining as to why the command for Jihad was given so soon. Following this, addressing them, it was stated: fleeing from duties is no path for escaping death. Death will come at its time, and when its time arrives, it will find every person out, even if they are sitting hidden inside extremely strong fortresses. Then, a specific mental confusion of the hypocrites was unveiled: these people's creed is not that whatever the Prophet does, he does only by God's command and under God's guidance. For this reason, if they achieve success, they attribute it to God; and if some fall occurs, they designate it the result of the Prophet's mismanagement—that he is not a sagacious leader, which is why he makes wrong estimates and wrong decisions whose results turn out wrong. (Consequently, it passed in Surah Al Imran that the hypocrites tried to place the entire responsibility for the defeat at Uhud upon the Holy Prophet PBUH—that this defeat occurred due to his mismanagement). From this, the point emerges clearly that these people neither believed that only God's will is operating in the universe, nor did they believe that every work of the Messenger is by God's command. Outwardly they acknowledged your Messengership, but in the interior, this very thought was hidden: that you do all works by your own opinion and strategy. To refute this delusion of theirs, the Prophet (PBUH) was instructed: clarify to them that whether success or failure, pain or joy, none of these things is from me but everything is from Allah alone—both because I do no work without God's command and because the true User of this universe is Allah, the One without partner, alone. Without His will, neither pain nor joy can reach anyone in this world. But the state of these people is that they do not even go near understanding any point.

'alam tara ilal-ladhina qila lahum kuffu aydiyakum wa aqimu-s-salata wa atu-z-zakata fa-lamma kutiba 'alayhimul-qitalu idha fariqum-minhum yakhshawnan-nasa ka-khash-yatillahi aw ashadda khash-yah wa qalu rabbana lima katabta 'alaynal-qitala law la akh-khartana ila ajalin qarib qul mata'ud-dunya qalil wal-akhiratu khayrul-liman-it-taqa wa la tuzhlamuna fatila'.

Champions of Speech, Hollow in Action: The state of the Muslims' oppression and helplessness in the disbelievers' regions during that era has passed in the previous verses. From these circumstances, the feeling of war was inevitable within the Muslims of Madinah. When the Muslims mentioned this feeling of theirs to the Prophet (PBUH), the hypocrites too expressed the passion for war with full zeal—in fact, to place a veil over their weaknesses, they expressed even more zeal. It is the rule that one whose action is weak falls into a kind of inferiority complex, due to which they have to take the support of boasting so that the secret of their cowardice is not outed to others. Consequently, the hypocrites too expressed great longings with the tongue, but the Prophet (PBUH) stopped them: wait for now and develop your connection with Allah, your organization, and your passion for spending through the observance of prayer and zakat. But when its time arrived and war was commanded, the entire zeal of these champions of the tongue cooled down; now they try to hide, and the terror that was in their hearts for humans was greater than the awe and fear that should be for God. They said in their hearts: "O God! Why did You give this command for war so soon? Why did You not give some more respite?" 'Qalu' (they said) here is expressing their mental state. Examples of this are many in Arabic and the Quran. It stated: tell them that the life of this world and its comfort and rest are merely for a few days. Why this restlessness for it? Eternal comfort is in the Hereafter, which is for those who fear Allah instead of those who fear humans. Gird your loins for that and be assured that you will receive the reward for every single bit of what you do. Not the slightest bit will be decreased for them.

Deep Suitability between Jihad and Prayer/Zakat: From this verse, the point emerges that there is an extremely deep suitability between the spirit of Islamic war and prayer/zakat. For those preparing to fight in God's path, the observance of 'iqamat-e-salat' and 'ita-e-zakat' is more necessary than weapons training. The sincerity, devotion, and discipline of order and obedience required in Jihad are best trained through prayer, and the passion for 'Infaq fi sabilillah' (spending in God's path) required for it is nurtured by the firm habit of 'ita-e-zakat'. Without these attributes, if a group rises for war, no reform can manifest from that war; it can only increase 'Fasad fil-Ardh' (corruption in the land). This is the wisdom why the emphasis on the observance and adherence to prayer was made even in the hardest circumstances of Islamic war. We are going to discuss this issue further in this Surah; therefore, we suffice with a hint here.

'ayna ma takunu yudrik-kumul-mawtu wa law kuntum fi burujim-mushayyadah wa in tushibhum hasanatuy-yaqulu hadhihi min 'indillahi wa in tushibhum sayyi'atuy-yaqulu hadhihi min 'indika qul kullum-min 'indillahi fa-ma-li-ha'ula'il-qawmi la yakaduna yafqahuna haditha'.

'Buruj' is the plural of 'Burj'. In its initial meaning, it was used for any prominent and clear thing, but then it became well-known for high buildings and fortresses. The attribute 'mushayyadah' with it encompasses both meanings of height and stability. 

Now the cause of these hypocrites' fear of death was clarified, and that creed was also stated which is the sole remedy for this fear. It stated: there is no escape from death for anyone; whoever's death is written at what hour, at what place, and in what form—it will come; even if a person sits hidden inside the strongest fortresses, death will find them out there too; for this reason, fearing it and fleeing from it is useless. A person should perform the duty that becomes mandatory upon them at any time with resolve and courage and leave the issue of death to God. It is not permissible for a person to avoid strategies and cautions because they have no right to test God, but simultaneously it is also not permissible for them to hold the presumption that they can save themselves from death through their strategies. Then, an indication was made toward another folly of the hypocrites which had a large role in nurturing this cowardice of theirs: that during this struggle of truth and falsehood, they did not consider all the mild and harsh circumstances that were arising as being from God; rather, they considered successes as being from God, but if some difficulty or some trial faced them, they attributed it to the Prophet's mismanagement—that he is not a sagacious leader, which is why he makes wrong estimates and wrong decisions whose results turn out wrong. (Consequently, it passed in Surah Al Imran that the hypocrites tried to place the entire responsibility for the defeat at Uhud upon the Holy Prophet PBUH—that this defeat occurred due to his mismanagement). From this, the point emerges clearly that these people neither believed that only God's will is operating in the universe, nor did they believe that every work of the Messenger is by God's command. Outwardly they acknowledged your Messengership, but in the interior, this very thought was hidden: that you do all works by your own opinion and strategy. To refute this delusion of theirs, the Prophet (PBUH) was instructed: clarify to them that whether success or failure, pain or joy, none of these things is from me but everything is from Allah alone—both because I do no work without God's command and because the true User of this universe is Allah, the One without partner, alone. Without His will, neither pain nor joy can reach anyone in this world. But the state of these people is that they do not even go near understanding any point.

79 to 80: 'ma ashabaka min hasanatin fa-minallahi wa ma ashabaka min sayyi'atin fa-min nafsika wa arsalnaka lin-nasi rasulaw-wa kafa billahi shahida. may-yuti'ir-rasula faqad ata'allaha wa man tawalla fa-ma arsalnaka 'alayhim hafizha'.

These verses are clarifying some summaries of the previous verse. First, addressing those who were attributing successes to Allah Almighty and failures to the Messenger of Allah (PBUH), it stated: the original reality is indeed that the manifestation of everything, good or evil, happens by God's will alone. Without His command and permission, nothing can manifest. But the difference between good and evil is that good manifests from the requirement of God's mercy, and evil is the consequence of a human's own deeds. From this perspective, evil relates to the human's own soul.

This reality should be noted here: Allah Almighty is Absolute Good. He created this world for His mercy. For this reason, the attribution of any Shirk (evil/partnership) to Him is contrary to His pure attributes. Whatever evil manifests, it manifests only from the misuse of the human's own choice. Allah Almighty has granted the human freedom within a specific circle. This freedom is a very great blessing of Allah Almighty. The foundation of all human honor is upon it. Due to it, the human will be deserving of reward and punishment in the Hereafter. If this freedom were not obtained by the human, there would be no difference between animal and human. But regarding this freedom, it must be remembered that it is not unlimited and unrestricted; rather, as we hinted, it is limited within a specific circle. Then, within this circle too, it is under God's will and His wisdom. Without God's permission and will, a human cannot fulfill any of their intentions. Good intentions too are fulfilled through His granting of success, and bad intentions too manifest only through His giving of respite. If Allah Almighty allows someone's bad intention to manifest, then from that perspective it is attributed to God—that its manifestation occurred by God's permission and will—but from the other perspective, it is the human's act because the intention for it was made by the human themselves.

The Wisdom of giving Respite to Evil: Then it is also a reality that when Allah Almighty gives respite for some evil of an individual or group to rise, He does so because some wisdom and benefit for His creation as a whole is kept in view in it. Sometimes, through this respite, the people of truth are tested so that their weaknesses are removed and their virtues flourish. Sometimes, it is to complete the proof over the people of falsehood and to fill their measure. Sometimes, Providence itself creates such circumstances whose objective is that what is suppressed within temperaments emerges. From this, virtues emerge, and the evils of those within whom evils are hidden also emerge.

Objection to any point of the Messenger necessitates Denial of his Messengership: After addressing the hypocrites, further on the Prophet (PBUH) was addressed: if these hypocrites are hesitant regarding your Messengership and do not consider your every word and deed as being from God, do not care for this point. Your Messengership is not in need of their testimony. God's testimony is sufficient for it. Whether these people believe or not, the only path to the obedience of Allah now is that people obey you. The obedience of God happens through the medium of the Messenger's obedience alone. Regarding those who turn away from you—you have not been sent as a guardian responsible for their guidance; leave them to their state; now the responsibility is not yours but their own.

If no confusion exists, there is no evil in the dispersion of pronouns: Here, the address occurred in the same context to both the hypocrites and the Messenger of Allah (PBUH). But in such instances, there is no harm in this type of address where there is no apprehension of confusion. Since the first point could clearly be said addressing the hypocrites—because it is the answer to their doubt—therefore no apprehension of confusion existed here. Then, in the second segment, regarding the point said addressing the Holy Prophet (PBUH)—if you reflect, the focus of the speech is in reality toward the hypocrites too, but Allah Almighty, as an expression of aversion, addressed His Messenger instead of addressing them. As if after this lack of appreciation for the Messenger from their side, they are no longer worthy of address such that a point regarding the Messenger be said addressing them.

81 to 82: 'wa yaquluna ta'atun fa-idha barazu min 'indika bayyata ta'ifatum-minhum ghayral-ladhi taqulu wallahu yaktubu ma yubayyituna fa-a'ridh 'anhum wa tawakkal 'alallahi wa kafa billahi wakila. a-fa-la yatadabbarunal-qur'ana wa law kana min 'indi ghayrillahi lawajadu fihi-khtilafan kathira'.

Benefit of Omitting the Subject: 'Ta'atun' (Obedience) is the predicate (khabar), its subject (mubtada') is omitted, and we have clarified that when the subject is omitted, the purpose is to give all the force to the predicate. The meaning is: when they are in the Prophet's assembly and the Prophet recites God's speech and His commands to them, they say at every point: "The head of submission is bowed." 'Bayyata yubayyitu' originally means to perform some act at night, but in its general usage, this word—divested of the restriction of night—is used for making a hidden plan, a secret consultation, or an opinion. Examples of words thus divested of their initial meaning are many in Arabic. 'Adh-ha' and 'Bata' are also used divested of the restriction of day and night in their general usage. Here the meaning is: in the Prophet's assembly, they bow the head of submission at every point, but when they depart from there, they hold consultations in their own assemblies against those verses and commands which they find contrary to their desires and their personal interests. Upon this attitude of theirs, the Quran first gave them a threat: that all these points of theirs are being recorded by Allah Almighty; they should not think that if they are whispering in secret, it is also hidden from God; one day this entire record will come before them. After that, the Prophet (PBUH) was instructed: you turn away from them and rely upon God; God's reliance is sufficient; these wretched people will not be able to harm the true religion at all through these whisperings; whatever they harm, they will harm only themselves. Then, the hypocrites were directed toward their contradiction of thought: on one hand, bowing the head of submission to the Quran and the Prophet's words, and on the other hand, making those same words of the Quran and the Prophet a target of objection—after all, what does this mean? Do they think that in this Quran, two wills are operating simultaneously? That some of its points are wise and sagacious, worthy of praise, and some points are contrary to wisdom and benefit, deserving of objection and criticism? Do they not reflect on the Quran? If they reflected, they would see for themselves that every point of the Quran is so stable and connected in its principles and branches that even the formulas of mathematics and Euclidean geometry cannot be so stable and connected. The creeds it teaches are linked and joined to each other in such a way that if you separate even one of them, the entire chain will be in disarray. The acts of worship and obedience it commands sprout from the creeds just as branches sprout from the trunk; the actions and morals it urges manifest from their principles just as natural and innate requirements manifest from a thing. From its collective teaching, the system of life that is formed manifests in the form of a 'solid structure' (bunyanun-marsush) whose every brick is joined to the other brick in such a way that separating any of them is not possible without a void being produced in the entire building. Just as the mutual harmony of the various components of this universe and their compatibility is an extremely clear testimony that a single will of the Living and Sustaining God is operating within it, and no one else is a partner in it; similarly, the mutual compatibility and harmony of the different parts of this beloved Book is an extremely clear proof that this is the revelation of a single Knowing and Wise God, and there is no intervention of any other Jinn or human in it. If different wills were operating in this universe, it would be in disarray. Similarly, if some other thought had infiltrated this beloved Book, it would have remained a dispersed register of contradictions and differences. 

This verse contains a great warning for the people of this era who, on one hand, accept the Quran as Allah's Book, and on the other hand, also make all its teachings that are contrary to their desires or their presumed benefits a target of criticism. Their conduct indicates that they too, like those hypocrites, believe the hand of other-than-God is also a partner with God in this Book; otherwise, after all, what does it mean that it is accepted as God's Book yet many of its points are also made the target of objection, criticism, desecration, and mockery.

'wa idha ja'ahum amrum-minal-amni awil-khawfi adha'u bihi wa law radduhu ilar-rasuli wa ila ulil-amri minhum la-'alimahul-ladhina yastanbitunahu minhum wa law la fadhlullahi 'alaykum wa rahmatuhu lattaba'tumush-shaytana illa qalila'.

Discussion on 'Ulul-Amr' passed under verse 59 of this Surah. The original meaning of "Istinbat" is to dig a well and take water out from it, and to reveal some hidden thing. Progressing from this meaning, it was used in the sense of reaching the bottom of a point and finding its reality.

Since the hypocrites were not well-wishers of the nation, they were very skillful in the matter of spreading rumors. Whatever point of security or danger reached them, in a moment it would spread through them among the people like a forest fire. In communal life, rumors can produce dangerous results even in general circumstances, and when the circumstances are of war—as they were then—their danger doubles. The hypocrites tried to take various benefits from these rumors, but specifically, they were their special weapon for lowering the morale of the Muslims, especially the weak Muslims. Sometimes they also tried to produce a wrong type of satisfaction among the Muslims through such rumors, and obviously, like improper anxiety, false satisfaction is also a dangerous thing.

The Correct Conduct regarding Rumors: It stated: if they were well-wishers of Allah, the Messenger, and the nation, then the correct method for them was that such points that came to their knowledge—instead of making them a means for producing sensation among the masses—they should present them before the Messenger and the 'Arbab-e-Hall o 'Aqd' (those in authority) of the Ummah so that those among them who possess deep understanding of affairs could determine their occasion and place and adopt the correct policy regarding them. 'Alima' also comes in the meaning of determining the occasion and place of a thing. We hinted toward this meaning of the word in Al-Baqarah under 'qad 'alima kullu unasim-mashrabahum' (and every tribe of the Children of Israel determined their own watering place). From 'wa law la fadhlullahi 'alaykum...', the hint emerges that even now there is an opportunity for them to recover. It is Allah's favor and kindness that He has not yet left them to walk headlong behind Satan, although this too is Satan's path.

The Importance of the Political System in Islam and the Status of the 'Ulul-Amr': From this verse, the importance and greatness of the political system in Islam are clarified: that the masses should make their 'Ulul-Amr' the reference point and confidant in matters of collective benefit and harm. It is not permissible to spread such things among the masses independently. Similarly, from this, the point also emerges that for the 'Ulul-Amr' in Islam, it is necessary that they be people possessing such understanding of both religion and the world that they can take steps according to the demands of Sharia and benefit in the arising circumstances and transactions.

84-85: 'fa-qatil fi sabilillahi la tukallafu illa nafsaka wa harridhil-mu'minina 'asallahu ay-yakuffa ba'sal-ladhina kafaru wallahu ashaddu ba'saw-wa ashaddu tankila. may-yashfa' shafa'atan hasanatay-yakul-lahu nashibum-minha wa may-yashfa' shafa'atan sayyi'atay-yakul-lahu kiflum-minha wa kanallahu 'ala kulli shay'im-muqita'.

We clarified the meaning of "shafa'" in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah: to join one thing with another. Progressing from this meaning, it was used in the sense of supporting and aiding a point or interceding in its favor. Here, two types of intercession are mentioned: 'shafa'ah hasanah' (good intercession) and 'shafa'ah sayyi'ah' (bad intercession). Good intercession is obviously that from which a true objective obtains support and strength. In contrast, bad intercession is that from which, instead of strength and support, that objective suffers harm. The hypocrites, instead of urging people toward the Quran's invitation to Jihad—as hinted above and as detail follows—were trying to lower people's morale; therefore, the Quran described their effort as 'shafa'ah sayyi'ah'.

'Muqit' means witness, guardian, and powerful.

You will recall that the start of this group of verses was with the subject: first the hypocrites made very loud talk of war and Jihad, but now when war has been commanded, they are hiding and also trying to lower others' morale. After that, other points of this series had come under discussion. Now the Prophet (PBUH) is being addressed: the responsibility upon you is only for your own soul; you yourself rise for war and urge the Muslims to rise; whoever is blessed with success will rise, and the responsibility for the one who does not rise is not upon you. Allah's works are not dependent on others; He Himself is the possessor of great power and the Giver of exemplary punishment to opponents. He will produce so much power within you and your sincere companions that through it alone the disbelievers' force will be broken. 

Saying something with the tongue in support of the Truth also earns reward: Following this, it stated: do not care for the machinations and rumor-mongering the hypocrites are doing to lower the Muslims' morale and stop them from Jihad. Those who open their tongues today in support and aid of the truth will receive its reward, and those who speak against it will suffer its punishment; God is watching everything and is powerful over everything.

Subject of Verses 86 to 100: The instruction given to the Prophet (PBUH) in verse 81 to turn away from the hypocrites could naturally have had the effect on the life-risking Companions that regarding anyone about whom they even had a suspicion that they were connected to the hypocrites, they would end all social links and greetings with them, and thus a form of social boycott would arise which was not yet desired in this stage. For this reason, it was necessary at this occasion that the Muslims be given such instructions that would keep them on moderation. They should remain alert toward the hypocrites but not end greetings and talk with them. Give them more opportunity for reform. Consequently, first of all, the Muslims were instructed: whoever greets you, respond to their greeting in a better way or at least return their greeting. Then, addressing those who held a very soft corner in their hearts for the hypocrites of the 'Abode of War' (Dar al-Harb)—and despite their anti-Islamic acts being clear, expected that if links were maintained with them, they would eventually become good Muslims—He stated: these people are not going to become Muslims; however, there is an apprehension that if you remain bound to them, they will sink you too. Then, He designated the touchstone for them to be considered sincere: that they migrate from their environment and join you. If they do not do so, then instead of maintaining a relationship with them, consider them enemies and allies of enemies; fight them. From this general command, He exempted only those Muslims who belong to such non-Muslim tribes with whom the Muslims have a treaty of peace, or there is no treaty but the Muslims of those tribes currently want to maintain their neutrality—neither ready to fight the Muslims along with their nation, nor finding the courage to fight their nation along with the Muslims. He stated: if they maintain their neutrality, do not interfere with you, and their way with you remains conciliatory, then you too should not take any aggressive step against them. Along with this, He also clarified that it is necessary that this neutrality be real and true. Some groups are such that they are apparently claimants of neutrality, but when the disbelievers' pressure falls upon them, they become ready for enmity against Islam. Regarding them, He stated: deal with them like enemies; if they do not cease aggressive action against you, then you too kill them. After this, He described the ruling for the Diyat (blood money) of Muslims killed by error or intentionally in the 'Abode of War' and issued a threat in extremely harsh words: whoever kills a Muslim intentionally, for them is the eternal punishment of Hell and God's wrath and His curse upon them. Simultaneously, He instructed the Muslims: before you attack any region of the disbelievers, first investigate well regarding the Muslims within it so that Muslims remain safe from your sword, and if someone expresses their faith to you by greeting you, do not refuse to accept them as a Muslim merely out of greed for wealth. After this, He urged all non-disabled Muslims toward Jihad in the path of God with both life and wealth, and urged all non-disabled Muslims toward migration from the 'Abode of War' and 'Abode of Kufr', and described their ranks and degrees. In this light, recite the following verses.

86-87: 'wa idha huyyitum bi-tahiyyatin fa-hayyu bi-ahsana minha aw rudduha innallaha kana 'ala kulli shay'in hasiba. allahu la ilaha illa huwa layajma'annakum ila yawmil-qiyamati la rayba fihi wa man ashdaqu minallahi haditha'.

'Hayyahu tahiyyatan' originally means to pray for someone's life. From this comes the prayerful phrase 'Hayyakallah', meaning "May Allah prolong your life." Since 'Salam' and other prayerful phrases of similar meaning also carry more or less this or a similar meaning, they are all included within the general meaning of the word.

Importance of Greeting (Salam) in Islamic Society: In every society, some such prayerful phrases are prevalent which the members of society use at the time of meeting each other as an initial introduction, expression of love and trust, sign of brotherhood and affection, and symbol of unity of thought and creed. From the viewpoint of social connection and link, they hold great importance. The members of society—no matter how much distance and alienation exists within them—become joined to each other through their medium as soon as they face each other, as if no strangeness or alienation existed within them. Among the Arabs, many words and phrases were well-known for this purpose. For example: 'Hayyakallah', 'Ahlan wa Sahlan wa Marhaba', etc. The word 'Salam' was also well-known. When the Islamic society manifested, then except for those phrases in which some taint of Shirk existed, all other pure phrases remained; however, 'Assalamu Alaikum' obtained the status of a specific Islamic symbol (Sha'irah). This phrase became, as it were, a distinguishing mark between the believer and the disbeliever. When one person said 'Assalamu Alaikum' before another and the other responded with 'Wa Alaikum Assalam', then effectively the difference of 'me and you' was removed and both became 'one soul in two bodies'; and not responding did not only mean that they did not accept the greeting, but it also meant that they did not acknowledge their Islam. Due to this importance of the word of greeting, at this occasion when the Prophet (PBUH) was instructed—as we hinted—to turn away from the hypocrites, the Muslims were simultaneously warned: when someone addresses you with a greeting and 'Tahiyyah', its Islamic and social right is that you respond to their greeting and 'Tahiyyah'; the highest method is to respond in a better way; if not this, then at least return their words to them. The need for this warning was so that the turn for a social boycott with the hypocrites does not arrive at this stage from the more passionate people. Especially, this aspect was very delicate: in some circumstances, its strike could also fall upon those who were not in reality hypocrites but more sensitive people would suspect them of hypocrisy for some reason.

This complexity was regarding those hypocrites who were in the 'Abode of Islam' (Dar al-Islam). Even more complex was the matter of those Muslims who were in the 'Abode of War' (Dar al-Harb). Among these Muslims too, as is coming ahead, were both hypocrites and sincere ones. Although the Quran appointed extremely clear touchstones for distinguishing between them, nonetheless the apprehension remained that some sincere Muslim might fall under the strike of the Muslims' sword; therefore, the Muslims were instructed further on in verse 94: investigate well regarding the Muslims of the region you attack; if someone greets you and thus expresses their religious brotherhood and affection with you, do not deny their being a Muslim without investigation.

In short, this matter of greeting and responding to greeting did not hold a formal status but was the foundation of connection and separation in the Islamic society; for this reason, the Quran described it with importance and warned that God is the Accountant of everything and everyone must give an account of their deeds and words on the Day of Resurrection.

After 'layajma'annakum', the preposition 'ila' (to), as we clarified this style elsewhere, is proof that some word in the meaning of 'driving', 'pushing', and 'taking' is omitted.

88-89: 'fa-ma lakum fil-munafiqina fi'atayni wallahu arkasahum bi-ma kasabu a-turiduna an tahdu man adhallallahu wa may-yudhlilillahu fa-lan tajida lahu sabila. waddu law takfuruna kama kafaru fa-takununa sawa'an fa-la tattakhidhu minhum awliya'a hatta yuhajiru fi sabilillahi fa-in tawallaw fa-khudhuhum waqtuluhum haythu wajadtumuhum wa la tattakhidhu minhum waliyyaw-wa la nashira'.

'Fi'atayni' is in the state of condition (Hal) from the genitive pronoun. For example, one would say: 'ma-laka qa'iman'. 'Rakasa-sh-shay'' means he overturned the thing; 'arkasahu', he turned it upside down; 'arkasa-sh-shay'', he returned the thing to its previous state.

Migration is the Touchstone for the Faith and Disbelief of Muslims in the 'Abode of Kufr': Now the mention is occurring of those hypocrites who, without any reasonable excuse, purely out of love for their relationships and kinships or property and assets, were avoiding migration and, despite the establishment of the 'Abode of Islam' in Madinah, were still lying in the 'Abode of Kufr' or 'Abode of War' as before. Since these people possessed no Shari excuse, their hypocrisy was clear; but some among the Muslims, who held kinships and relationships or family and tribal affiliations with them, were very soft regarding them. Their desire was that they not only be left to their state but that links be maintained with them—gradually these people would become true and firm Muslims. The Quran warned the people of this thought: those thinking on this pattern are thinking wrongly. Now these hypocrites are not ones to advance toward Islam; the step they had taken toward Islam—out of love for the world, they have pulled back their raised step, in punishment for which Allah, according to His Sunnah, has pushed them back into that same Kufr in which they were before. Those who have fallen under the strike of God's law and His Sunnah can no longer come to the right path; no matter how much one wishes, it is impossible for them to find the path. It stated: you expect their guidance while their state is that they desire to take you back into that same Kufr in which they themselves are; therefore, until they migrate, do not maintain any relationship with them. This migration alone is the touchstone of their faith and Islam. If they avoid it, then consider them enemies and allies of enemies and capture and kill them wherever you find them.

'illal-ladhina yashiluna ila qawmim-baynakum wa baynahum mithaqun aw ja'ukum hashirat shuduruhum ay-yuqatilukum aw yuqatilu qawmahum wa law sha'allahu lasallatahum 'alaykum fa-la-qatalukum fa-in-i'tazalukum fa-lam yuqatilukum wa alqaw ilaykumus-salama fa-ma ja'alallahu lakum 'alayhim sabila'.

'Hashira hashiran' means to be helpless, to be constricted, to be low-spirited; 'hashira-r-rajulu dhaqa shadruhu', his breast was constricted, he abandoned courage. "Salm" means submission and obedience and entrustment and surrender. "Ilqa' al-salm" refers to throwing down the shield before someone, kneeling, surrendering, and requesting peace from them.

Now the ruling is described for those who are exempt from the aforementioned command of capture and killing. These are two types of people. One, those who belong to some nation and tribe with whom the Muslims have a treaty of peace. The sparing of such people's lives was done purely in respect of the treaty. This is because until the maintenance of the treaty, capturing or killing any individual of theirs would be a breach of covenant, regardless of whether they are a disbeliever or a hypocrite. Second, those who, due to their weakness and low-spiritedness, are neither ready to fight the Muslims nor prepared to join the Muslims to fight their own nation; the benefit of giving them respite was clarified: this attitude of neutrality from such weak people is also a boon. After all, it could also have happened that He gave them courage and they stood up to fight you as open enemies; so as long as they remain withdrawn from interfering with you, do not fight you, and maintain a conciliatory way with you, you too should not take any action against them.

We have discussed the word "Fitna" in detail in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah; here it refers to the aggressive and oppressive actions of the disbelievers aimed at turning Muslims away from Islam and, in their presumption, erasing Islam. The word "Sultan" appears in the Quran in the sense of evidence or proof, as well as in the sense of authority and power. There are several parallels for this second meaning, such as: "I had no authority [sultan] over you" (Ibrahim: 22), and "Whoever is killed unjustly - We have given his heir authority [sultanan] over the killer" (Al-Isra: 33).

Ruling on False Claimants of Neutrality: This verse points to those false neutrals who, by claiming Islam, wanted to remain safe from the danger of Muslims while also remaining secure within their own people. These people were apparently claimants of neutrality, but this neutrality was merely for show. When pressured by their nation, they would participate in the mischief intended against Islam and the Muslims. Regarding them, it was stated that they are not worthy of the concession described for the aforementioned group. Rather, they too fall under the ruling of open enemies. If they do not cease their opposition, do not adopt a conciliatory attitude toward you, and do not restrain their hands, then capture and kill them wherever you find them. God has granted you clear authority to capture and kill them.

92-93. Protection of the Lives of Sincere Muslims in the Abode of War (Dar al-Harb): Although the commands mentioned above were sufficient to remove confusion between believers and hypocrites, there were many sincere Muslims in the regions of the disbelievers who personally desired migration but were prevented by compulsions. In the event of war, there was an apprehension that they might suffer harm at the hands of the Muslims themselves. For this reason, the Quran clarified the gravity of the crime of killing a believer and provided such clear commands that no God-fearing Muslim is left with any room for carelessness or negligence in this matter. First, it stated: 'It is not for a believer to kill a believer except by error. And whoever kills a believer by error - then [the penalty is] the freeing of a believing slave and blood money [diyat] presented to the victim's family unless they give [it as] charity.' Then, it clarified this summary: 'But if the victim was from a people hostile to you and he was a believer - then [the penalty is] the freeing of a believing slave. And if he was from a people with whom you have a treaty - then a blood money payment presented to his family and the freeing of a believing slave. And whoever does not find [one or cannot afford it] - then [instead], a fast for two months consecutively, [as] repentance to Allah. And Allah is ever Knowing and Wise.' Meaning, if the slain Muslim is a member of a hostile nation or tribe, then freeing a believing slave is sufficient. But if he belongs to a covenanted nation or tribe, then both paying blood money and freeing a believing slave are necessary. If a person does not have the means to free a slave, they must fast for two consecutive months. It stated: this is the repentance prescribed by Allah, and Allah is Knowing and Wise.

The Gravity of the Crime of Intentional Murder: After describing the rulings for accidental killing, it stated regarding intentional murder: 'And whoever kills a believer intentionally - his recompense is Hell, wherein he will abide eternally, and Allah has become angry with him and has cursed him and has prepared for him a great punishment.'

The punishment described here for intentional murder is exactly the same punishment described in the Quran for staunch disbelievers. Every Muslim's heart trembles upon reading this verse. To understand the cause of this severity, one must remember that a Muslim's greatest right over another Muslim is the respect for his life. If a Muslim takes the life of another Muslim, it means he has violated the greatest of the 'rights of servants' (huquq al-'ibad), for which no form of compensation or reform remains, because the person whose right he violated has departed from the world, and for the reform of the rights of servants, making amends for the past is indispensable. Then, another aspect is very important: this is the case of killing a Muslim who, due to being surrounded in the Abode of Disbelief and War, was already deprived of those protections a Muslim enjoys in the Abode of Islam. If he could hope for any good regarding his religion and his self, it could only be from Muslims. Now, if a Muslim kills him intentionally in a place where he does not even have the protection of Islamic law, then obviously no one can be more oppressed than such a victim, and no one can be a greater oppressor than such a killer!

Commands based on Custom (Urf) change with the change of circumstances: We discussed some aspects of the issue of blood money (khun-baha) in the exegesis of Surah Al-Baqarah. Islam gave the 'Ma'ruf' (custom) of the Arabs the status of law in this matter. And we have written elsewhere that matters related to custom change with the passage of time and circumstances while keeping their original purpose intact. For example, in place of camels and goats for blood money, cash can be given, and the amount of cash can change with the change in economic conditions. Determining the nature of this change is the work of the authorities of Ijtihad, and parallels of the predecessors' Ijtihads exist in this chapter.

Substitute for a Slave: In the verse under discussion, there is a command to free a slave as repentance. Since slavery has ended in this era—and we clarified elsewhere that its ending occurred exactly according to the intent of Islam—the question arises: what should a person do in this era who has the means to free a slave but slaves are not available, and the Sharia has not specified a substitute? In our view, the substitute in this era is charity (sadaqah) in proportion to the price of a slave. And if this charity is spent on paying off the debts of poor and destitute Muslims and releasing their mortgaged houses and goods, then God willing, this method will not be against the intent of the Sharia.

The segment 'tawbatan minallahi wa kanallahu 'aliman hakima' is also particularly worth considering. We have written elsewhere that when a verbal noun (maf'ul) appears without a verb like this, the intention is to emphasize it with special force and resolve. Here, along with blood money, the instruction to free a slave—or fast for two consecutive months if unable—was emphasized as the repentance appointed by the Knowing and Wise God; let no one find it heavy, nor violate it. Killing a believer, even if by error, is a great sin. To wash away this sin, blood money alone is not enough; a slave must also be freed, and if unable, two consecutive months must be fasted so that every stain of this sin is washed from the heart. Effectively, in such a grave matter, verbal repentance is not enough; it must be accompanied by its supporting acts.

Further Arrangement for the Protection of Muslims in the Abode of War: From the perspective of protecting Muslims trapped in the Abode of War, further instruction was given: 'O you who have believed, when you go forth [to fight] in the cause of Allah, investigate; and do not say to one who gives you [a greeting of] peace "You are not a believer," aspiring for the goods of worldly life; for with Allah are many acquisitions. You [yourselves] were like that before; then Allah conferred His favor upon you, so investigate. Indeed Allah is ever, with what you do, Acquainted.' Meaning: when you set out to attack a region, conduct a full investigation regarding the Muslims within that region—where they are and in what state—so they remain safe from your attack. Furthermore, it was stated: if a Muslim greets you to testify to his faith, do not deny his faith out of greed for spoils. Those seeking spoils should remember that God has vast stores of acquisitions. After that, He stated: it should not be forgotten that until yesterday, your state was the same. You too, like these oppressed ones, were surrounded by the enclosure of the disbelievers. Now that Allah has blessed you with the free and open atmosphere of the Abode of Islam, you should not fall into any sense of superiority. You should take action only after thorough investigation. If anyone allowed negligence and laxity in this matter or killed a Muslim out of greed for spoils, remember that God is aware of your every deed.

The ultimate limit of respect for a believer's life is what manifests from this verse. Even during actual war in the Abode of War, if a person greets or recites the Kalimah to express his faith, it is not permissible for a Muslim to raise a sword against him without investigation. Although such investigation is extremely difficult in the emergency conditions of war, and there is an apprehension that the enemy might exploit it, this instruction was fully observed in Islamic expeditions. In one expedition, a Companion was careless in this matter, and the Prophet (PBUH) warned him so severely that the hearts of the listeners were terrified. The fact is that the original objective of Islamic expeditions was not to achieve conquests and gather spoils but, as passed above, to rescue oppressed Muslims from the grasp of the disbelievers. When this was the original objective, every risk could be taken for it, but how could it be tolerated that a Muslim's life be put in danger?

95-96. Now all non-disabled Muslims are urged toward Jihad. Effectively, the discourse has returned to where it began in verse 71, before the mention of those shrinking from Jihad. It stated: those Muslims who have no reasonable excuse yet still do not rise for Jihad should remember that they will not be equal in reward with God to those who are performing Jihad today with both life and wealth. Although both groups are worthy of good reward from Allah in the sense that both are sincere to Islam and neither is a hypocrite or ill-wisher of Islam, nonetheless, the rank of the Mujahideen is very high with God. For them is a great reward with God.

This verse clarified, along with the motivation for Jihad, that although those Muslims who are not practically participating in Jihad without any excuse or compulsion cannot be equal in rank and status, they cannot be considered hypocrites for not practically participating. This is because not participating in Jihad is hypocrisy only when a person shrinks from it, discourages others, or remains sitting at home despite a general call to arms. If this is not the case, then Jihad is certainly an excellence that every person should have the passion to achieve, but its status is only that of a degree of excellence; it is not among the conditions of faith such that whoever does not achieve it is considered a hypocrite. The words 'and to all Allah has promised the best [reward]' point toward this reality. This warning was necessary because the way the hypocrites were rebuked above regarding this Jihad and the way Muslims were instructed to be alert toward them could have created an impression in some people that their angle of vision would change even regarding those sincere Muslims who were very true Muslims but had not yet participated in Jihad. This verse, on one hand, urged them toward Jihad, and on the other hand, clarified that these are sincere Muslims—no one should have bad suspicions about their sincerity; there is a reward for them too according to their rank and status.

97-100. 'Indeed, those whom the angels take [in death] while they are wronging themselves - [the angels] will say, "In what [condition] were you?" They will say, "We were oppressed in the land." [The angels] will say, "Was not the earth of Allah spacious [enough] for you to emigrate therein?" For those, their refuge is Hell - and evil it is as a destination.'

In 'tawaffahumul-mala'ikah', the word 'mala'ikah' (angels) appears in the plural just as it appeared in Surah Al Imran. We explained the reason for its plural form under 'fa-nadat-hul-mala'ikah'. Sometimes the plural aims only to express the species. 'zhalimi anfusihim' (wronging themselves) is a state (Hal). Wronging one's own soul here refers to remaining in the Abode of Disbelief despite having the ability to migrate, thus putting one's faith at risk. 'fi-ma kuntum' (In what were you?) is a question of the nature of rebuke and reprimand. 'Mustadh'af' means helpless, compelled, suppressed, or dominated. 'Muraghaman' refers to a place where a person can go out to.

Command of Migration for all non-disabled Muslims: All non-disabled Muslims still residing in the Abode of War were urged to migrate, and this is effectively the final warning for them. Its preamble was raised thus: those who, despite claiming Islam, still reside in the Abode of Disbelief without any severe compulsion or Shari excuse—if death comes to them in that state, the angels will ask: "In what state did you remain?" They will reply: "We were helpless and compelled." The angels will respond: "Was God's earth not spacious enough for you to migrate?" Then He stated: the refuge of such people will be Hell, and it is a wretched destination.

Following this, He mentioned those who are truly helpless and disabled. He stated: 'Except for the oppressed among men, women and children who cannot devise a plan nor are they directed to a way.' Only those men, women, and children will be considered disabled with God who neither possess the power to devise any strategy nor is any path opening for them; these are the ones Allah will hopefully overlook. After that, He encouraged those rising with resolve for migration: 'And whoever emigrates for the cause of Allah will find on the earth many [alternative] locations and abundance. And whoever leaves his home as an emigrant to Allah and His Messenger and then death overtakes him - his reward has already become incumbent upon Allah. And Allah is ever Forgiving and Merciful.' He assured that for the great reward of migration, it is not necessary for a person to actually reach the Abode of Migration; it is sufficient that the person sets out from home with the intention of migrating to Allah and the Messenger. If death comes immediately or they are killed, there will be no reduction in their reward. Their reward has become mandatory upon Allah.

Some Realities regarding Migration: From these verses, the following points regarding migration emerge. First, not every relocation is migration. Migration is for a Muslim to leave a place where remaining on their religion and faith has become a life-risking task, and move to a place where they expect to be able to protect their faith. Second, if the Abode of Islam exists, the path to it is open, and there is no severe compulsion, then migrating from such a place to the Abode of Islam is mandatory; otherwise, such a person's faith is not authoritative. Third, in the matter of migration, not every excuse is an excuse; an authoritative excuse is for a person to be so helpless that no strategy is formed by them nor is any path opening for them. Even in such compulsion, protecting their faith is nonetheless mandatory upon them, even if they have to take refuge in a cave like the People of the Cave. Fourth, the reward for migration is what it is in the Hereafter, but in the world too, a special escort from Allah Almighty is provided for the emigrant. God's earth opens paths for them, and means and resources are provided for them from the unseen. Fifth, in this path, even the first step holds the status of the destination. If the intention is pure and the resolve firm, then even if death comes to the emigrant upon leaving home, the reward of migration has become mandatory for them.

Subject of Verses 101 to 104: Further on, the form of congregational prayer during the dangers of war, known as 'Salat al-Khawf', is described. From this arrangement of prayer—especially congregational prayer—alongside the mention of Jihad, several realities manifest, some of which we will point out.
